examples.code:
; Hello World Program
; Demonstrates basic string output using DOS interrupt 21h

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    MSG DB 'Hello, World!$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    ; Initialize data segment
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display string using DOS function 09h
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, MSG
    INT 21H
    
    ; Return to DOS
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: String must end with $ for DOS function 09h
MOV AX, @DATA loads the data segment address into AX, then copies to DS
AH=09h is DOS function to display string at DS:DX
LEA loads effective address of MSG into DX

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize DS before using string data
Not ending the string with '$' - function 09h uses $ as terminator
Using AH=01h instead of AH=09h for string output

    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: .MODEL SMALL defines a small memory model (code + data < 64KB)
STACK 100H reserves 256 bytes for the stack
Always initialize DS before accessing data segment variables

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize DS register
Not ending program with INT 21h/AH=4Ch
Confusing .DATA and .CODE segment order

examples.code:
; Addition Program
; Demonstrates adding two numbers and storing the result

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    NUM1 DB 25
    NUM2 DB 17
    RESULT DB ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Load first number into AL
    MOV AL, NUM1
    
    ; Add second number
    ADD AL, NUM2
    
    ; Store result (CF will be set if overflow occurred)
    MOV RESULT, AL
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: ADD modifies the destination (AL), source (NUM2) unchanged
ADD sets CF=1 if unsigned overflow occurred
RESULT receives AL, which holds the sum

commonMistakes: Using ADD on two memory operands directly (one must be register)
Not checking CF after unsigned addition to detect overflow
Assuming result fits in destination when adding large numbers

Character returned in AL is stored to CHAR variable

Confusing AH and AL when setting up INT 21h functions

examples.code:
; Demo Program - Arithmetic and Comparison
; Shows basic arithmetic operations and conditional logic

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    A DB 10
    B DB 3
    RESULT DB ?
    MSG1 DB 'A > B$'
    MSG2 DB 'A <= B$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Compare A and B
    MOV AL, A
    CMP AL, B
    
    ; If AL > B, skip the subtraction
    JA SKIP_SUB
    
    ; Subtract: A - B
    SUB AL, B
    MOV RESULT, AL
    JMP DONE
    
SKIP_SUB:
    ; Add: A + B
    ADD AL, B
    MOV RESULT, AL
    
DONE:
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CMP performs subtraction but doesn't store result, only updates flags
JA jumps if unsigned AL > B (CF=0 and ZF=0)
SUB sets OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF based on result

commonMistakes: Confusing JA (unsigned) with JG (signed)
Forgetting that CMP only affects flags, doesn't store result
Using CMP then wrong jump condition

examples.code:
; Procedure Example
; Demonstrates procedure call with stack operations

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    RESULT DW ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Call the procedure
    MOV AX, 5
    PUSH AX              ; Pass parameter on stack
    CALL SQUARE
    POP CX               ; Clean up stack
    MOV RESULT, DX
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP

; Procedure to calculate square
; Parameter: AX (value to square)
; Returns: DX (result)
SQUARE PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    
    MOV AX, [BP+4]      ; Get parameter
    MUL AX               ; AX * AX -> DX:AX
    MOV DX, AX           ; Store result in DX
    
    POP BP
    RET
SQUARE ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH decrements SP by 2 and copies AX to stack
CALL pushes return address and jumps to SQUARE
MUL without operand uses AX as implicit operand
RET pops return address and jumps back to caller

commonMistakes: Not balancing stack - push/pop mismatch causes corruption
Forgetting to preserve BP register in procedure
Using AX instead of [BP+4] to access stack parameters

examples.code:
; For Loop Example
; Counts from 1 to 10 using LOOP instruction

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    COUNT DB 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Initialize counter
    MOV CX, 10          ; Loop 10 times
    
LOOP_START:
    INC COUNT           ; Increment count variable
    
    ; Could do more work here
    
    LOOP LOOP_START     ; CX--, if CX != 0 jump to LOOP_START
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CX is the counter register used implicitly by LOOP
LOOP decrements CX, jumps if CX != 0
INC adds 1 to operand, doesn't affect CF

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize CX before LOOP
Modifying CX inside the loop unexpectedly
Using LOOP with CX=0 (will loop 65536 times!)

examples.code:
; If-Else Example
; Demonstrates conditional branching

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    X DB 5
    Y DB 10
    MSG_EQ DB 'X equals Y$'
    MSG_NE DB 'X not equal to Y$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Compare X and Y
    MOV AL, X
    CMP AL, Y
    
    ; If equal, display equality message
    JE EQUAL
    
    ; Else branch - display not equal message
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, MSG_NE
    INT 21H
    JMP DONE
    
EQUAL:
    MOV AH, 09H
    LEA DX, MSG_EQ
    INT 21H
    
DONE:
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CMP sets flags based on AL - Y (result not stored)
JE jumps if ZF=1 (equal), otherwise continues
JMP unconditionally transfers control to label

commonMistakes: Forgetting that CMP doesn't store result
Using JE for signed comparison when JA/JB needed
Not using JMP to skip else block after then block executes

examples.code:
; Recursive Procedure Example
; Calculates factorial using recursion

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    RESULT DW ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Calculate 5! = 120
    MOV AX, 5
    PUSH AX
    CALL FACTORIAL
    POP AX
    MOV RESULT, DX
    
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP

; FACTORIAL procedure
; Input: n on stack (word)
; Output: DX = n!
FACTORIAL PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    
    MOV CX, [BP+4]      ; Get n
    
    ; Base case: if n <= 1, return 1
    CMP CX, 1
    JLE BASE_CASE
    
    ; Recursive case: n * factorial(n-1)
    PUSH CX
    DEC CX
    PUSH CX
    CALL FACTORIAL
    POP CX               ; Clean up parameter
    
    ; Multiply result by n
    MOV AX, DX
    MOV DX, [BP+4]
    MUL DX               ; AX * DX -> DX:AX
    
    POP BP
    RET
    
BASE_CASE:
    MOV DX, 1
    POP BP
    RET
FACTORIAL ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Stack frame preserves return address and BP
Base case prevents infinite recursion
Each recursive call pushes new return address

commonMistakes: Forgetting base case - causes infinite recursion and stack overflow
Not cleaning up stack parameters before RET
Losing track of which registers are preserved across calls

examples.code:
; Bubble Sort Example
; Sorts an array of bytes using bubble sort

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ARRAY DB 5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2
    N DB 6
    TEMP DB ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Outer loop: N-1 passes
    MOV CH, N
    DEC CH               ; Passes = N-1
    
OUTER_LOOP:
    MOV CL, N
    DEC CL               ; Comparisons per pass = N-1
    LEA SI, ARRAY        ; Point to start of array
    
INNER_LOOP:
    MOV AL, [SI]         ; Get current element
    CMP AL, [SI+1]       ; Compare with next
    JBE NO_SWAP          ; Skip if AL <= next
    
    ; Swap: exchange [SI] and [SI+1]
    MOV TEMP, AL
    MOV AL, [SI+1]
    MOV [SI], AL
    MOV AL, TEMP
    MOV [SI+1], AL
    
NO_SWAP:
    INC SI
    LOOP INNER_LOOP
    
    DEC CH
    JNZ OUTER_LOOP
    
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: [SI] uses register indirect addressing
JBE jumps if unsigned AL <= [SI+1]
Three MOVs required to swap values in memory

commonMistakes: Not decrementing comparison count in inner loop correctly
Using signed jump (JLE) instead of unsigned (JBE) for array indices
Forgetting that LOOP uses CX, corrupting outer loop counter

examples.code:
; Flag Instructions and Stack Operations
; Demonstrates flag manipulation and stack behavior

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    counter DW 0
    result DB 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Flag Instructions ===
    ; Clear carry flag for arithmetic
    CLC                 ; CF = 0
    
    ; Set direction flag for string operations (forward)
    CLD                 ; DF = 0 (SI/DI auto-increment)
    
    ; Example: Adding numbers without carry interference
    MOV AL, 0FFh        ; AL = 255
    ADC AL, 01h         ; AL = 0, CF was cleared so no add of 1
    
    ; Set carry flag for special conditions
    STC                 ; CF = 1
    
    ; === Stack Operations ===
    ; The stack grows toward lower addresses
    ; SP points to the current top of stack
    
    ; Push registers to preserve values
    PUSH AX             ; SP = SP - 2, [SP] = AX
    PUSH BX             ; SP = SP - 2, [SP] = BX
    PUSH CX             ; SP = SP - 2, [SP] = CX
    
    ; Use registers freely now
    MOV AX, 1234h
    MOV BX, 5678h
    ADD AX, BX          ; AX = 68ACh
    
    ; Pop in reverse order (LIFO)
    POP CX              ; CX = original CX, SP = SP + 2
    POP BX              ; BX = original BX
    POP AX              ; AX = original AX
    
    ; === Using BP for Stack Frame Access ===
    PUSH BP             ; Save caller's BP
    MOV BP, SP          ; BP points to current stack frame
    
    ; Access parameters at [BP+4] (return address) and [BP+6] (first param)
    MOV AX, [BP+4]      ; Get return address
    
    POP BP              ; Restore caller's BP
    
    ; === LAHF - Load Flags into AH ===
    ; LAHF copies SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF to AH bits 0-4
    LAHF                ; AH = flags register lower byte
    
    ; Check carry flag
    TEST AH, 01h        ; Test CF bit (AH bit 0)
    JNZ CARRY_SET       ; Jump if carry flag was set
    
CARRY_SET:
    ; Exit program
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CLC clears the carry flag to prepare for unsigned arithmetic
ADC adds with carry - since CF=0, this acts like regular ADD
PUSH decrements SP by 2 and copies source to [SS:SP]
Stack grows DOWN in memory - SP decreases with each push
POP increments SP by 2 and copies from [SS:SP] to destination
Must pop in reverse order (LIFO) to restore correctly
BP register provides stable reference to access stack parameters
LAHF loads lower 8 flags into AH for testing or saving

commonMistakes: Confusing SP and BP - SP tracks top of stack, BP is frame pointer
Popping in wrong order corrupts values - always use LIFO (reverse push order)
Forgetting that PUSH/POP operate on 16-bit words, not bytes
Not preserving BP before modifying it in a procedure

examples.code:
; Stack Instructions Demonstration
; Shows PUSH, POP, PUSHA, POPA, PUSHF, POPF operations

.386
.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    saved_ax DW 0
    saved_flags DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Basic PUSH/POP ===
    MOV AX, 1234h
    PUSH AX             ; Save AX
    MOV AX, 5678h       ; Modify AX
    POP AX              ; Restore AX (AX = 1234h again)
    
    ; === PUSHA/POPA - Push/Pop All General Registers ===
    ; Order: AX, CX, DX, BX, SP, BP, SI, DI
    MOV AX, 0AAAAh
    MOV CX, 0CCCCh
    MOV DX, 0DDDDh
    MOV BX, 0BBBBh
    MOV BP, 0EEEEh
    MOV SI, 0FFFFh
    MOV DI, 01234h
    
    PUSHA                ; Push all 8 registers (16 bytes)
    
    ; Modify all registers
    MOV AX, 0000h
    MOV CX, 0000h
    MOV DX, 0000h
    MOV BX, 0000h
    
    POPA                 ; Restore all registers
    ; AX = AAAA, CX = CCCC, etc.
    
    ; === PUSHF/POPF - Save and Restore Flags ===
    ; Set some flags via operations
    MOV AX, 0001h
    CMP AX, 0002h        ; ZF=0, CF=1 (AX < 2)
    PUSHF                ; Push flags to stack
    
    ; Clear flags
    CLC                 ; CF = 0
    CLD                 ; DF = 0
    
    ; Modify flags
    MOV BX, 0000h
    SUB BX, 0001h        ; CF = 1
    
    POPF                 ; Restore original flags (ZF=0, CF=1)
    
    ; === Complete Stack Frame Example ===
    PUSH BP              ; Save old BP
    MOV BP, SP           ; BP = current stack frame
    
    ; Stack now looks like:
    ; [BP]     = old BP
    ; [BP+2]   = return address
    ; [BP+4]   = first parameter (if any)
    
    SUB SP, 4           ; Allocate 4 bytes for local variables
    ; Local variables at [BP-2] and [BP-4]
    
    MOV [BP-2], AX      ; Store local var 1
    MOV [BP-4], BX      ; Store local var 2
    
    ; Restore stack frame
    MOV SP, BP           ; Deallocate locals
    POP BP               ; Restore old BP
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH preserves register values on stack for later restoration
PUSHA pushes all 8 general registers in specific order
Order: AX, CX, DX, BX, SP, BP, SI, DI (SP value is what was before decrement)
PUSHF saves current flag state to stack - useful before operations that modify flags
POPF restores flags from stack - restores exact flag state
Establishing stack frame with BP allows access to parameters and local variables
SUB SP, N allocates N bytes of local variable space

commonMistakes: PUSHA/POPA require .386 directive - MASM won't assemble without it
Forgetting that PUSH always decrements SP by 2 (for 16-bit)
POPA restores in reverse order but skips SP (restore order is DI, SI, BP, BX, DX, CX, AX)
Not balancing stack - too many PUSHes without POPs causes stack overflow
Using POP to restore flags when PUSHF wasn't called first

examples.code:
; Parameter Passing Demonstration
; Shows both register and stack parameter passing

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    result DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Method 1: Register Parameter Passing ===
    MOV AL, 5           ; First parameter in AL
    MOV BL, 3           ; Second parameter in BL
    CALL MULTIPLY_REG
    ; Result in AX = 15
    MOV result, AX
    
    ; === Method 2: Stack Parameter Passing ===
    PUSH 3              ; Push second parameter
    PUSH 5              ; Push first parameter
    CALL ADD_STACK
    ADD SP, 4           ; Clean up stack (2 params × 2 bytes)
    ; Result in AX = 8
    MOV result, AX
    
    ; === Caller-Saved Register Preservation ===
    PUSH CX             ; Save CX (caller saved)
    PUSH DX             ; Save DX (caller saved)
    
    MOV AL, 10
    CALL MULTIPLY_REG   ; CX, DX preserved across call
    
    POP DX              ; Restore DX
    POP CX              ; Restore CX
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h

; Register-based multiplication
; AL = first param, BL = second param
; Returns: AX = product
MULTIPLY_REG PROC
    MUL BL              ; AX = AL * BL
    RET
MULTIPLY_REG ENDP

; Stack-based addition
; Parameters at [BP+6] and [BP+4]
; Returns: AX = sum
ADD_STACK PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    
    MOV AX, [BP+6]      ; Get first parameter (pushed second)
    ADD AX, [BP+4]      ; Add second parameter (pushed first)
    
    POP BP
    RET                 ; Return address popped automatically
ADD_STACK ENDP

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Register parameter passing is fast but limited - only available registers
Stack parameters pushed in REVERSE order so first param is at higher address
CALL pushes return address before jumping to procedure
ADD SP, 4 cleans up stack (removes the 2 pushed parameters)
Caller-saved: calling code preserves registers it needs across calls
Stack frame: BP+6 is first param, BP+4 is second (due to reverse push order)
RET pops return address from stack and jumps back to caller

commonMistakes: Forgetting to clean up stack parameters after stack-based call (ADD SP, N)
Incorrect parameter offset calculation - [BP+4] for first, [BP+6] for second
Confusing caller-saved vs callee-saved register conventions
Not preserving registers when required by calling convention
Parameters pushed in wrong order causing offset confusion

examples.code:
; Array Manipulation Examples
; Shows how to define, address, and traverse arrays

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Byte array (8 elements)
    byteArray DB 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80
    
    ; Word array (4 elements)
    wordArray DW 1000h, 2000h, 3000h, 4000h
    
    ; Uninitialized byte array
    buffer DB 16 DUP(?)
    
    sum DW 0
    foundIndex DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Accessing Array Elements ===
    ; Direct indexing with constant offset
    MOV AL, byteArray[0]    ; AL = 10 (first element)
    MOV AL, byteArray[3]    ; AL = 40 (fourth element, offset = 3)
    
    ; Using register as index (SI, DI, BX)
    MOV SI, 2
    MOV AL, byteArray[SI]   ; AL = 30 (third element)
    
    ; === Word Array Indexing ===
    ; IMPORTANT: Offset is always in bytes, even for word arrays!
    MOV SI, 0
    MOV AX, wordArray[SI]       ; AX = 1000h (first word)
    
    MOV SI, 2                    ; SI = 2 (NOT 1 for second element!)
    MOV AX, wordArray[SI]        ; AX = 2000h (second word)
    
    MOV SI, 4
    MOV AX, wordArray[SI]        ; AX = 3000h (third word)
    
    ; === Searching an Array ===
    MOV CX, 8               ; Array length
    MOV SI, 0               ; Index
    MOV AL, 50              ; Value to find
    
search_loop:
    CMP AL, byteArray[SI]
    JE found
    INC SI
    LOOP search_loop
    
    ; Not found - SI = 8
    JMP done
    
found:
    MOV foundIndex, SI
    
done:
    ; === Summing an Array ===
    MOV CX, 8               ; Counter
    MOV SI, 0               ; Index
    XOR AX, AX              ; AX = 0 (accumulator)
    
sum_loop:
    ADD AL, byteArray[SI]
    INC SI
    LOOP sum_loop
    
    MOV sum, AX
    
    ; === Finding Max Value ===
    MOV CX, 8
    MOV SI, 0
    MOV AL, 0               ; Max = 0
    
max_loop:
    CMP byteArray[SI], AL
    JNA not_max
    MOV AL, byteArray[SI]
not_max:
    INC SI
    LOOP max_loop
    ; AL now contains maximum value (80)
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: byteArray[SI] uses SI as offset from array start - each element is 1 byte
Word array requires doubling index: element N at offset N*2
wordArray[2] accesses second element (2000h), not first (would be wordArray[0])
Loop compares each element to target value, exits when found or end reached
AL accumulator sums all bytes - note potential overflow for large arrays

commonMistakes: Forgetting that word array indices must be doubled (element 3 at offset 6, not 3)
Using byte array index for word array causing misalignment
Assuming byteArray[SI] modifies SI - it does NOT, just adds SI to base address
Memory-to-memory moves not allowed: MOV array[2], array[5] is ILLEGAL
Not using XOR AX, AX to clear accumulator - must initialize before ADD

examples.code:
; Number Input and Output
; Reads and prints multi-digit integers

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    prompt DB 'Enter a number (max 5 digits): $'
    buffer DB 7, ?, 7 DUP(?)
    number DW 0
    outputMsg DB 'You entered: $'
    newline DB 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Display prompt
    LEA DX, prompt
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    
    ; Read string of digits
    LEA DX, buffer
    MOV AH, 0Ah
    INT 21h
    
    ; Convert ASCII string to number
    CALL ASCII_TO_NUM
    MOV number, AX
    
    ; Print result
    LEA DX, newline
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    
    LEA DX, outputMsg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    
    ; Print the number
    MOV AX, number
    CALL PRINT_NUM
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h

; ========================================
; Convert ASCII string in buffer to number
; Input: buffer contains digits ending with CR
; Output: AX = number
; ========================================
ASCII_TO_NUM PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    PUSH SI
    PUSH BP
    
    MOV SI, OFFSET buffer + 2  ; Point to first digit
    MOV CL, buffer[1]           ; Length in CL
    XOR CH, CH                 ; CX = length
    XOR AX, AX                 ; Result = 0
    MOV BP, 10                 ; Multiplier
    
convert_loop:
    CMP CX, 0
    JE convert_done
    
    MOV DL, [SI]              ; Get digit
    SUB DL, '0'               ; Convert ASCII to value
    
    ; AX = AX * 10 + digit
    MUL BP                    ; AX = AX * 10
    ADD AL, DL                ; Add digit
    
    INC SI
    LOOP convert_loop
    
convert_done:
    POP BP
    POP SI
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
ASCII_TO_NUM ENDP

; ========================================
; Print number in AX
; Algorithm: Divide by 10, push remainders, pop and print
; ========================================
PRINT_NUM PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    
    CMP AX, 0                 ; Handle zero specially
    JNE print_nonzero
    MOV DL, '0'
    MOV AH, 02h
    INT 21h
    JMP print_done
    
print_nonzero:
    XOR CX, CX                ; Digit counter
    
next_digit:
    CMP AX, 0
    JE print_digits
    
    MOV DX, 0
    MOV BX, 10
    DIV BX                    ; AX = quotient, DX = remainder
    
    ADD DL, '0'              ; Convert to ASCII
    PUSH DX                   ; Save digit
    INC CX
    JMP next_digit
    
print_digits:
    CMP CX, 0
    JE print_done
    
    POP DX                    ; Get digit
    MOV AH, 02h
    INT 21h                   ; Print character
    LOOP print_digits
    
print_done:
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
PRINT_NUM ENDP

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: buffer[1] contains the actual length of input (after function 0Ah reads)
MUL BP with BP=10 multiplies AX by 10 without destroying other registers
Special case: number is 0, just print '0'
DIV BX divides DX:AX by BX - quotient in AX, remainder in DX
Digits pushed to stack in reverse order, then popped to print in correct order
This is classic use of stack for reversal: LIFO gives reversed order

commonMistakes: Not converting ASCII '0'-'9' to values by subtracting '0' before arithmetic
Forgetting that DIV uses DX:AX - must clear DX before dividing AX by byte
Not handling the special case of number = 0 (would print nothing)
Buffer overflow: not reserving enough space in input buffer for digits
Confusing buffer format: buffer[0]=max, buffer[1]=actual, buffer[2]=first char

examples.code:
; Extending 8-bit Programs to 16-bit
; Shows how to preserve values and work with larger numbers

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    temp1 DW 0
    temp2 DW 0
    result DW 0
    value8 DB 200          ; 8-bit value
    value16 DW 60000       ; 16-bit value

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Problem: MUL overwrites AX and DX ===
    MOV AL, 100            ; 8-bit multiplicand
    MOV BL, 50             ; 8-bit multiplier
    
    PUSH AX               ; Save AX (contains AL)
    PUSH DX               ; Save DX (might be used elsewhere)
    
    MUL BL                ; AX = AL * BL = 5000 (1388h)
    ; AX = 1388h, DX = 0 (no high part overflow)
    
    POP DX                ; Restore DX
    POP AX                ; Restore AX
    
    ; === 16-bit Multiplication ===
    MOV AX, 1234h          ; Multiplicand
    MOV BX, 5678h          ; Multiplier
    
    PUSH AX               ; Save AX before MUL (MUL overwrites it)
    PUSH DX               ; Save DX (high part of result)
    
    MUL BX                ; DX:AX = AX * BX
    ; DX:AX now holds 32-bit result
    ; Result = 0x0BDC0F80 (approx 3.16 billion)
    
    POP DX                ; Restore DX
    POP AX                ; Restore AX
    
    ; === Division preserving registers ===
    MOV AX, 1234h         ; Dividend
    MOV CX, 100h          ; Divisor
    
    PUSH AX               ; Save dividend
    PUSH DX               ; Save any high part
    
    XOR DX, DX            ; Clear DX for 16-bit division
    DIV CX                ; AX = quotient, DX = remainder
    ; AX = 0x12 (18), DX = 0x34 (52)
    
    POP DX                ; Restore DX
    POP AX                ; Restore AX
    
    ; === Using Data Segment for preservation ===
    MOV AX, 0ABCDh        ; Important value
    MOV temp1, AX         ; Store in data segment
    
    ; Do something that modifies AX
    MOV BX, 1234h
    MUL BX                ; AX overwritten
    
    ; Restore original value
    MOV AX, temp1          ; AX = ABCDh again
    
    ; === 8-bit to 16-bit extension ===
    MOV AL, value8         ; AL = 200
    CBW                   ; Sign-extend AL to AX (AX = 0200h or FFE0h if negative)
    ; CBW: if AL < 128, AH = 00; if AL >= 128, AH = FF
    
    ; Now we can safely do 16-bit operations
    MOV BX, 1000h
    ADD AX, BX            ; AX = 0200h + 1000h = 1200h
    
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH before MUL preserves values that MUL would overwrite
MUL BL multiplies AL by BL, result in AX
16-bit MUL: DX:AX = AX * BX (32-bit result)
MUL always uses AX and another register - result splits across AX and DX
DIV overwrites AX and DX - must preserve if needed
XOR DX, DX clears DX before DIV to ensure high word of dividend is 0
Data segment storage preserves values across operations that modify registers
CBW sign-extends AL to AX - essential before 16-bit operations on 8-bit values

commonMistakes: Forgetting that MUL overwrites AX (and DX for 16-bit) - must preserve first
Not clearing DX before 16-bit DIV - DX:AX must be full dividend
Confusing CBW (sign-extend) with just moving AL to AH (which zeros AH)
Using stack for long-term storage - stack is for temporary preservation
Forgetting that PUSH/POP are 16-bit operations - cannot push 8-bit registers directly

examples.code:
; Floating Point Example
; Demonstrates x87 FPU instructions: FLD, FST, FADD, FSUB

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    val1 REAL4 3.14       ; 32-bit float
    val2 REAL4 2.71       ; 32-bit float
    result REAL4 ?        ; Store result here
    temp REAL8 ?          ; 80-bit intermediate (ST register)

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Basic FP Register Loading ===
    ; FLD pushes a value onto the FP stack (ST0)
    FLD val1              ; ST0 = 3.14, ST1-ST7 = empty
    
    ; === Floating Point Addition ===
    ; FADD: ST0 = ST0 + source
    FLD val2              ; ST0 = 2.71, ST1 = 3.14
    FADD val1             ; ST0 = 2.71 + 3.14 = 5.85, ST1 = 3.14
    ; Note: FADD with memory adds to ST0
    
    ; === Store Result ===
    FSTP result           ; Store ST0 to result, pop stack
    ; ST0 now = 3.14 (was ST1)
    
    ; === Subtraction ===
    ; FSUB: ST0 = ST0 - source
    FLD val1              ; ST0 = 3.14
    FSUB val2             ; ST0 = 3.14 - 2.71 = 0.43
    FSTP result           ; Store and pop
    
    ; === Stack Operations ===
    ; ST(0) is top, ST(1)-ST(7) form the stack below
    FLD val1              ; ST0 = 3.14
    FLD val2              ; ST0 = 2.71, ST1 = 3.14 (pushed)
    FADD                 ; Without operand: ST0 = ST0 + ST1, pop
    ; Now ST0 = 5.85, ST1 = empty (was popped)
    FSTP result           ; Store and pop
    
    ; === FST without pop ===
    FLD val1              ; ST0 = 3.14
    FST temp              ; Copy ST0 to temp (no pop)
    ; ST0 still = 3.14
    FSTP result           ; Now pop
    
    ; === FADDP/FSUBP (add/subtract with pop) ===
    FLD val1              ; ST0 = 3.14, ST1 = empty
    FLD val2              ; ST0 = 2.71, ST1 = 3.14
    FADDP                ; ST0 = 2.71 + 3.14 = 5.85, ST1 popped
    FSTP result           ; Store result
    
    ; === Clean up FP register stack ===
    ; FLDCW, FINIT, etc. for proper initialization
    
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: FLD loads a value onto the FP stack, pushing ST0 down
FADD ST0 = ST0 + source (source can be memory or another stack register)
FSTP stores and pops - ST0 goes to result, ST1 becomes new ST0
FSUB performs ST0 = ST0 - source
FADD without operands uses ST0 and ST1 - convenient for stack operations
FST copies without popping - useful for preserving values
FADDP adds and then pops the stack - reduces stack depth by 1

commonMistakes: Confusing FST (store without pop) with FSTP (store and pop) - can cause stack overflow
Assuming FP registers behave like integer registers - they are stack-based
Forgetting that FADD without operands uses ST0 and ST1, not memory
Not checking for NaN or infinity conditions in floating point results
Using 80-bit long double precision but only reserving 64-bit storage

examples.code:
; Recursive Factorial with Stack Frame Management
; Demonstrates recursive call stack and stack unwinding

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    result DW ?

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Calculate 5! = 120
    MOV AX, 5
    PUSH AX              ; Push parameter (n) onto stack
    CALL FACTORIAL
    ADD SP, 2            ; Clean up stack (remove parameter)
    MOV result, DX
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP

; FACTORIAL Procedure
; Calculates n! recursively using the stack to preserve
; the current value of n for each recursive level
;
; Stack Frame Layout:
;   [BP+4] = parameter n (pushed by caller or recursive call)
;   [BP+2] = return address (pushed by CALL)
;   [BP]   = saved BP (pushed by this procedure)
;\; Recursive Call Flow for factorial(5):
;   Call factorial(5) -> pushes return addr, n=5
;     Call factorial(4) -> pushes return addr, n=4
;       Call factorial(3) -> pushes return addr, n=3
;         Call factorial(2) -> pushes return addr, n=2
;           Call factorial(1) -> pushes return addr, n=1
;             Base case: return 1
;           Multiply: 1 * 2 = 2, return 2
;         Multiply: 2 * 3 = 6, return 6
;       Multiply: 6 * 4 = 24, return 24
;     Multiply: 24 * 5 = 120, return 120
;
FACTORIAL PROC
    PUSH BP               ; Save caller's BP
    MOV BP, SP            ; Set up new stack frame
    
    ; Get parameter n from stack at [BP+4]
    MOV CX, [BP+4]
    
    ; ===== BASE CASE =====
    ; If n <= 1, we're done - return 1
    CMP CX, 1
    JLE BASE_CASE
    
    ; ===== RECURSIVE CASE =====
    ; Stack grows as we recurse deeper:
    ;   Each call pushes: BP, return address, parameter n
    ;   Stack grows TOWARD LOWER addresses (SP decreases)
    
    ; Save current n for later multiplication
    PUSH CX               ; Save current n
    
    ; Decrement n and push for recursive call
    DEC CX                ; n = n - 1
    PUSH CX               ; Push new parameter
    CALL FACTORIAL        ; Recursive call - factorial(n-1)
    
    ; ===== STACK UNWINDING =====
    ; After returning from factorial(n-1):
    ;   - DX contains factorial(n-1) result
    ;   - Return address has been popped
    ;   - Our stack frame is restored
    ;   - CX still has original n (we pushed it)
    
    ; Clean up the parameter we pushed
    ADD SP, 2
    
    ; Restore original n
    POP CX                ; CX = original n
    
    ; Multiply: result = n * factorial(n-1)
    ; DX currently has factorial(n-1)
    MOV AX, DX            ; AX = factorial(n-1)
    MOV DX, CX            ; DX = n
    MUL DX                ; AX * DX -> DX:AX = n * factorial(n-1)
    
    ; DX now contains the final result
    ; (AX holds low word, DX holds high word for larger numbers)
    
    POP BP                ; Restore caller's BP
    RET                   ; Return to caller
    
BASE_CASE:
    ; Return 1 (0! = 1 and 1! = 1)
    MOV DX, 1
    POP BP                ; Restore BP
    RET                   ; Return to caller
FACTORIAL ENDP

END MAIN

annotations.text: PUSH saves BP so we can restore caller's frame after procedure completes
MOV BP, SP establishes new stack frame - BP now references this call's frame
[BP+4] accesses the parameter n passed on the stack
Base case: n <= 1 means we stop recursing and return 1
PUSH CX saves current n before recursive call - essential for later multiplication
CALL pushes return address and jumps to FACTORIAL - stack depth increases
STACK UNWINDING: After recursive call returns, stack pops unwinds back to this frame
DEC CX reduces n for the recursive call, pushing n-1 onto stack
MUL computes n * factorial(n-1) - result in DX:AX, we use DX for final result
POP BP and RET restore caller's frame and return address - stack unwinds fully

commonMistakes: Forgetting the base case - causes infinite recursion and stack overflow
Not preserving the current n value before recursive call - loses n during unwinding
Forgetting to clean up stack parameters (ADD SP, 2) before returning
Using DX after MUL without checking if result exceeds 16 bits
Not establishing proper stack frame with PUSH BP / MOV BP, SP
Accessing parameters at wrong offset ([BP+6] instead of [BP+4])

examples.code:
; String Move Instructions: MOVSB and MOVSW
; Demonstrates copying data between memory locations using string instructions

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    source      DB 'Hello, World! This is a test string.', 0
    dest        DB 80 DUP(?)
    word_src    DW 1000h, 2000h, 3000h, 4000h, 5000h
    word_dst    DW 5 DUP(?)
    msg_src     DB 'Source string: $'
    msg_dst     DB 13, 10, 'Destination string: $'
    newline     DB 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX            ; ES must be set for string ops

    ; === Part 1: Copy String Using REP MOVSB ===
    ; Ensure direction is forward (DF = 0)
    CLD                     ; Clear direction flag - SI and DI increment

    ; Set up source and destination pointers
    LEA SI, source          ; SI = offset of source
    LEA DI, dest            ; DI = offset of destination

    ; Calculate length (find terminating 0)
    MOV CX, 0               ; Counter for length
    MOV AL, 0               ; Search for null terminator
count_loop:
    CMP [SI + CX], AL
    JE found_null
    INC CX
    CMP CX, 80
    JNE count_loop
found_null:

    ; Copy the string using REP MOVSB
    ; REP repeats CX times: MOVSB copies [DS:SI] to [ES:DI], increments both
    REP MOVSB               ; Copy CX bytes

    ; === Part 2: Copy Word Array Using REP MOVSW ===
    LEA SI, word_src        ; SI = source word array
    LEA DI, word_dst        ; DI = destination word array
    MOV CX, 5               ; Copy 5 words
    REP MOVSW               ; Each MOVSW copies 2 bytes, increments SI and DI by 2

    ; === Demonstrate Direction Flag Effect ===
    ; Forward direction (CLD): DF=0, SI/DI increment
    CLD
    LEA SI, source
    LEA DI, dest
    MOV CX, 5
    REP MOVSB               ; SI and DI both increase

    ; Backward direction (STD): DF=1, SI/DI decrement
    STD
    LEA SI, source[40]      ; Point to end of source (last valid position)
    LEA DI, dest[35]       ; Point to end of destination
    MOV CX, 5
    ; Note: When using STD, must start from END and copy backwards
    ; This is less common but useful for backward string operations
    REP MOVSB               ; SI and DI both decrease

    ; Reset to forward direction
    CLD

    ; === Practical Example: Copy Buffer ===
    MOV AL, '*'             ; Fill character
    LEA DI, dest            ; Destination
    MOV CX, 40              ; Fill 40 bytes
    REP STOSB               ; Fill with '*' (will cover original copy)

    ; Exit program
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CLD clears direction flag so SI and DI increment after each string operation
LEA loads effective address without reading memory - SI gets offset of source
REP prefixes repeat the string instruction CX times, automating the copy loop
MOVSB: [ES:DI] = [DS:SI], then SI=SI+1 and DI=DI+1 (or -1 if DF=1)
MOVSW copies a word (2 bytes) instead of a byte - SI and DI increment by 2
STD sets direction flag so SI and DI decrement after each string operation
When DF=1, string instructions auto-decrement SI and DI instead of incrementing
REP MOVSB repeats the byte copy - one instruction replaces an entire loop
REP STOSB fills memory with value in AL - demonstrates store string instruction

commonMistakes: Forgetting to set ES before string operations - MOVSB/MOVSW use ES:DI, not DS:DI
Not setting direction flag correctly - CLD for forward, STD for backward processing
Confusing MOVSB with MOVSW - MOVSB copies bytes, MOVSW copies words (2 bytes)
Starting backward copy at wrong address - STD copies from high to low addresses
Assuming REP clears CX - CX must be set before REP prefix
Forgetting that string instructions auto-modify SI and DI - don't increment them manually

examples.code:
; String Load Instructions: LODSB and LODSW
; Demonstrates loading string data into AL/AX using LODSB/LODSW

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    message     DB 'ASSEMBLY LANGUAGE PROGRAMMING'
    buffer      DB 26 DUP(?)
    upper_count DW 0
    vowel_count DW 0
    vowels      DB 'AEIOU'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX

    ; === Part 1: Count Uppercase Letters ===
    CLD                     ; Ensure forward direction
    LEA SI, message         ; SI points to string
    MOV CX, 26              ; Length of string
    XOR BX, BX              ; BX = uppercase counter

count_upper:
    LODSB                   ; AL = [DS:SI], SI = SI + 1
    CMP AL, 'A'
    JL not_upper
    CMP AL, 'Z'
    JG not_upper
    INC BX                  ; It's uppercase
not_upper:
    LOOP count_upper

    MOV upper_count, BX     ; Store result (should be 26)

    ; === Part 2: Count Vowels in String ===
    CLD
    LEA SI, message         ; Reset SI
    MOV CX, 26              ; Reset counter
    XOR DX, DX              ; DX = vowel counter

count_vowels:
    LODSB                   ; Load next character
    MOV BH, AL              ; Save character in BH

    ; Check if it's a vowel (A, E, I, O, U)
    LEA DI, vowels          ; DI points to vowel string
    MOV CX, 5               ; 5 vowels to check
    MOV AH, 0               ; Clear AH

check_vowel:
    CMP AL, [DI]           ; Compare with vowel
    JE found_vowel          ; If match, it's a vowel
    INC DI                  ; Next vowel
    LOOP check_vowel

    JMP next_char           ; Not a vowel

found_vowel:
    INC DX                  ; Increment vowel count

next_char:
    CMP CX, 0               ; Check if we checked all vowels
    LOOP count_vowels

    MOV vowel_count, DX

    ; === Part 3: Copy String Using LODSB ===
    LEA SI, message         ; Source pointer
    LEA DI, buffer          ; Destination pointer
    MOV CX, 26              ; Length

copy_loop:
    LODSB                   ; Load from source into AL
    MOV [DI], AL            ; Store to destination
    INC DI                  ; Must manually increment DI
    LOOP copy_loop          ; LODSB doesn't modify DI

    ; === Part 4: Load Words (16-bit values) ===
    LEA SI, buffer          ; SI points to our copied buffer
    MOV CX, 13              ; 26 bytes = 13 words

load_words:
    LODSW                   ; AX = [DS:SI], SI = SI + 2
    ; Process word in AX here
    ; For now just loop
    LOOP load_words

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: LODSB loads byte from [DS:SI] into AL, then increments SI by 1
Compare to check if character is between 'A' and 'Z' (uppercase ASCII range)
LODSB loads one character at a time for processing - common pattern
AH is cleared so AL contains just the character for comparison
LODSB only modifies SI, NOT DI - must manually increment DI yourself
LODSW loads word from [DS:SI] into AX, increments SI by 2
Important: LODSW increments by 2 for word alignment

commonMistakes: Thinking LODSB modifies DI - it only modifies SI
Forgetting to increment DI when copying: LODSB loads to AL, then you must store it
Confusing LODSB with MOVSB - LODSB reads into AL, MOVSB copies between memory
Using LODSB in a REP prefix - LODSB is not typically used with REP (why would you load CX times?)
Not resetting SI when reusing the same source string
Forgetting CLD before string operations if previous code might have set STD

examples.code:
; String Store Instructions: STOSB and STOSW
; Demonstrates storing AL/AX values to memory using STOSB/STOSW

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    buffer      DB 100 DUP(?)
    word_buffer DW 50 DUP(?)
    fill_char   DB '*'
    header      DB '=== String Store Demo ===', 13, 10, '$'
    footer      DB 13, 10, '=== Demo Complete ===', 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX              ; ES needed for STOS operations

    ; === Part 1: Fill Buffer with Character Using REP STOSB ===
    CLD                     ; Clear direction flag
    LEA DI, buffer          ; DI = destination buffer
    MOV AL, '*'             ; Character to store
    MOV CX, 50              ; Store 50 times
    REP STOSB               ; Fill [ES:DI] with AL, DI increments

    ; === Part 2: Initialize Word Buffer with Value ===
    CLD
    LEA DI, word_buffer     ; DI = word buffer
    MOV AX, 0FFFFh          ; Value to store in each word
    MOV CX, 25              ; Store 25 words
    REP STOSW               ; Store AX to [ES:DI] 25 times, DI increments by 2

    ; === Part 3: Build String Character by Character ===
    CLD
    LEA DI, buffer          ; Output buffer

    ; Add 'H'
    MOV AL, 'H'
    STOSB                   ; [ES:DI] = AL, DI = DI + 1

    ; Add 'e'
    MOV AL, 'e'
    STOSB

    ; Add 'l'
    MOV AL, 'l'
    STOSB

    ; Add 'l'
    MOV AL, 'l'
    STOSB

    ; Add 'o'
    MOV AL, 'o'
    STOSB

    ; Null terminate
    MOV AL, 0
    STOSB

    ; === Part 4: Copy-and-Fill Pattern ===
    ; Common pattern: initialize destination then fill with data
    CLD
    LEA DI, buffer          ; Target
    MOV AL, ' '             ; Fill with spaces
    MOV CX, 80              ; 80 characters
    REP STOSB               ; Clear buffer to spaces

    ; Now write specific data into initialized buffer
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV AL, 'A'
    STOSB                   ; buffer[0] = 'A'
    MOV AL, 'B'
    STOSB                   ; buffer[1] = 'B'
    MOV AL, 'C'
    STOSB                   ; buffer[2] = 'C'

    ; === Part 5: Reverse Copy Using STOSB ===
    ; Copy string but store in reverse order
    LEA SI, buffer          ; Source (where we just wrote ABC)
    MOV CX, 3               ; 3 characters to copy
    LEA DI, buffer[50]      ; Destination (different location)

reverse_copy:
    MOV AL, [SI]            ; Load from source
    STOSB                   ; Store to destination
    INC SI                  ; Move forward in source
    LOOP reverse_copy       ; LODSB not used - manual SI handling needed

    ; === Part 6: Practical - Clear Screen Buffer ===
    ; Simulate clearing a 80x25 text screen (2000 characters)
    CLD
    LEA DI, buffer          ; Screen buffer
    MOV AL, ' '             ; Space character (clear)
    MOV CX, 2000            ; Full screen
    REP STOSB               ; Clear 2000 characters

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: REP STOSB repeats: AL stored to [ES:DI], DI incremented, CX decremented, until CX=0
STOSB: [ES:DI] = AL, then DI = DI + 1 (or -1 if DF=1)
STOSW stores AX (16 bits) to [ES:DI], increments DI by 2
REP STOSW useful for clearing/initalizing word arrays quickly
Building string one character at a time - STOSB updates DI automatically
Common pattern: first fill buffer with spaces, then write specific data
STOSB only modifies DI, not SI - must manage SI manually for source
Practical use: clearing screen buffer to spaces before writing new content

commonMistakes: Forgetting to set ES register - STOS uses ES, not DS
Confusing STOSB with LODSB - STOS stores FROM AL to memory, LODSB loads TO AL
Not setting direction flag - DF=0 for forward, DF=1 for backward storage
Forgetting that STOSB/STOSW only modify DI, not SI
Using wrong value in AL/AX before STOS - must load value before storing
Assuming REP STOS clears CX automatically - CX must be set before REP

examples.code:
; REP Prefixes Demonstration
; Shows REP, REPE, and REPNE prefix usage with string instructions

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    source      DB 'ABCDEFGHIJ', 0
    dest        DB 20 DUP(?)
    word_src    DW 100h, 200h, 300h, 400h, 500h
    word_dst    DW 5 DUP(?)
    str1        DB 'HELLO'
    str2        DB 'HELLO'
    str3        DB 'WORLD'
    buffer      DB 'TEST STRING FOR SEARCHING', 0
    result_msg  DB 'Result: $'
    match_msg   DB 'MATCH!', 13, 10, '$'
    nomatch_msg DB 'NO MATCH', 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX

    ; === Part 1: REP MOVSB - Block Copy ===
    CLD
    LEA SI, source
    LEA DI, dest
    MOV CX, 10              ; Copy 10 bytes
    REP MOVSB               ; Repeat MOVSB CX times

    ; === Part 2: REP MOVSW - Word Block Copy ===
    LEA SI, word_src
    LEA DI, word_dst
    MOV CX, 5               ; Copy 5 words
    REP MOVSW               ; Each MOVSW copies 2 bytes

    ; === Part 3: REPE CMPSB - Compare While Equal ===
    ; Compare str1 and str2 (should be equal)
    LEA SI, str1
    LEA DI, str2
    MOV CX, 5               ; Compare 5 bytes
    REPE CMPSB              ; Compare while equal (ZF=1)
    ; After REPE CMPSB:
    ;   If CX=0 and ZF=1: All bytes matched
    ;   If CX>0 and ZF=0: Mismatch found at position (original_CX - CX)
    JZ strings_equal
    ; Not equal - CX shows position of mismatch
    JMP compare_done

strings_equal:
    ; CX=0 means all 5 bytes matched
    ; This path would execute for HELLO == HELLO

compare_done:

    ; === Part 4: REPE CMPSB - Find Difference ===
    ; Compare str1 and str3 (should not be equal)
    LEA SI, str1
    LEA DI, str3
    MOV CX, 5
    REPE CMPSB
    ; After REPE CMPSB:
    ;   CX=0 and ZF=1: Strings are equal
    ;   CX>0 and ZF=0: First mismatch found
    JNZ strings_differ
    ; Strings are equal (HELLO == WORLD? No, so this won't happen)
    JMP done_comparing

strings_differ:
    ; CX now holds position of first mismatch
    ; str1[0]='H', str3[0]='W' - they differ!

done_comparing:

    ; === Part 5: REPNE SCASB - Search for Value ===
    ; Find first 'S' in buffer
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV CX, 25              ; Search up to 25 bytes
    MOV AL, 'S'             ; Character to find
    REPNE SCASB             ; Scan while [DI] != AL
    ; After REPNE SCASB:
    ;   If CX=0: Reached max length without finding
    ;   If CX>0: DI points to first 'S' (or past it)
    JZ found_S
    ; Not found in 25 bytes
    JMP search_done

found_S:
    ; Found 'S' at position (25 - CX)
    ; DI now points to 'S' in buffer

search_done:

    ; === Part 6: REPNE SCASB - Find Null Terminator ===
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV CX, 0FFFFh          ; Max search length
    XOR AL, AL              ; AL = 0 (null character)
    REPNE SCASB             ; Search for null
    ; After: DI points past the null (or CX=0 if no null found)

    ; === Part 7: REP STOSB - Fill Memory ===
    LEA DI, dest
    MOV AL, 'X'             ; Fill character
    MOV CX, 20              ; Fill 20 bytes
    REP STOSB               ; Fill with 'X'

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: REP repeats the string instruction CX times: decrements CX, executes instruction, repeats until CX=0
REP prefix works with MOVS and STOS - repeats the operation CX times
REPE repeats CMPSB while ZF=1 (equal) and CX>0 - exits when mismatch or CX=0
REPE (Repeat While Equal) = REPZ - same prefix, different mnemonic
After REPE: CX=0 means all matched; CX>0 means mismatch at position (original_CX - CX)
REPNE repeats SCASB while ZF=0 (not equal) and CX>0 - used for searching
REPNE (Repeat While Not Equal) = REPNZ - same prefix, different mnemonic
After REPNE SCASB: ZF=1 means found (AL matched byte at DI); ZF=0 means not found (CX=0)
Common use: find null terminator by searching for 0 in buffer
REP STOSB fills memory with a value - one instruction replaces a fill loop

commonMistakes: Forgetting to set CX before REP prefix - CX controls repetition count
Confusing REPE with REPNE - REPE exits on mismatch (ZF=0), REPNE exits on match (ZF=1)
Not checking ZF after REPE/REPNE - the result is in the flags, not in a register
Forgetting that REPE/REPNE exit when either CX=0 OR condition changes
Using REP with LODSB - this would load the same byte CX times into AL (usually not useful)
Not setting direction flag (DF) before string operations - DF controls increment/decrement

examples.code:
; CMPSB and CMPSW Demonstration
; Shows string comparison using CMPSB and CMPSW

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    str_a       DB 'APPLE'
    str_b       DB 'APPLE'
    str_c       DB 'APPLF'
    str_d       DB 'BANANA'
    word_a      DW 1234h, 5678h, 9ABCh
    word_b      DW 1234h, 5678h, 9ABCh
    word_c      DW 1234h, 5678h, 9DEFh
    result      DB 'Comparison result: $'
    equal_msg   DB 'EQUAL', 13, 10, '$'
    nequal_msg  DB 'NOT EQUAL', 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX

    ; === Part 1: CMPSB - Compare Byte Strings ===
    CLD
    LEA SI, str_a
    LEA DI, str_b
    MOV CX, 5               ; Compare 5 bytes

compare_loop:
    CMPSB                     ; Compare [DS:SI] with [ES:DI], set flags
    ; After CMPSB: SI=SI+1, DI=DI+1
    JNE not_equal            ; If ZF=0, mismatch found
    LOOP compare_loop
    ; If we reach here, CX=0 and all bytes matched
    JMP strings_equal

not_equal:
    ; Mismatch found at position (5 - CX - 1) = position of difference
    ; CX is remaining count, str_a[5-CX-1] differs from str_b[5-CX-1]
    JMP done_comparing

strings_equal:
    ; All 5 bytes of str_a and str_b are equal

done_comparing:

    ; === Part 2: REPE CMPSB - Compare While Equal ===
    LEA SI, str_a
    LEA DI, str_c            ; str_c differs at last byte
    MOV CX, 5
    REPE CMPSB               ; Compare while equal
    ; After REPE CMPSB:
    ;   CX=0 and ZF=1: All equal
    ;   CX>0 and ZF=0: Found difference at position (5 - CX)
    JZ all_equal_c
    ; str_a and str_c differ
    JMP part3

all_equal_c:
    ; This won't execute because str_c differs

part3:
    ; === Part 3: CMPSW - Compare Word Strings ===
    CLD
    LEA SI, word_a
    LEA DI, word_b
    MOV CX, 3               ; Compare 3 words

word_compare_loop:
    CMPSW                     ; Compare word at [DS:SI] with [ES:DI], set flags
    ; After CMPSW: SI=SI+2, DI=DI+2
    JNE words_not_equal
    LOOP word_compare_loop
    ; All 3 words are equal
    JMP words_equal

words_not_equal:
    ; Found word difference - SI and DI already advanced
    JMP part4

words_equal:
    ; word_a and word_b are identical

part4:
    ; === Part 4: Find Mismatch Position ===
    LEA SI, str_d            ; BANANA
    LEA DI, str_a            ; APPLE
    MOV CX, 5
    XOR BX, BX              ; Position counter

find_diff_loop:
    CMPSB
    JNE diff_found
    INC BX                  ; Next position
    LOOP find_diff_loop
    JMP done_finding

diff_found:
    ; BX now holds position of first mismatch
    ; str_d[BX] != str_a[BX]
    ; B=66h vs A=41h at position 0

done_finding:

    ; === Part 5: Case-Insensitive String Compare ===
    ; Compare 'hello' with 'HELLO' (convert to uppercase first)
    LEA SI, str_a            ; APPLE
    LEA DI, str_a            ; Same string - just demo annotations
    MOV CX, 5
    ; In real scenario, you'd convert both to same case first
    REPE CMPSB              ; Check if exactly equal

    ; === Part 6: Using Flags After CMPSB ===
    LEA SI, str_a
    LEA DI, str_d
    CMPSB                     ; Compare first byte
    ; ZF=0 because 'A'(41h) != 'B'(42h)
    ; CF=0 because 41h < 42h (unsigned)

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CMPSB compares byte at [DS:SI] with byte at [ES:DI] and sets flags like SUB
CMPSB doesn't store result - only updates flags (like CMP but reads from memory)
After CMPSB, SI and DI are automatically incremented by 1
CMPSB sets ZF=1 if bytes are equal, ZF=0 if different
REPE CMPSB repeats comparison while bytes are equal (ZF=1) and CX>0
REPE exits when bytes differ (ZF=0) or CX reaches 0
CMPSW compares word at [DS:SI] with word at [ES:DI], increments SI/DI by 2
CMPSW is useful for comparing arrays of 16-bit values efficiently
After CMPSB: ZF=1 means equal, ZF=0 means different; CF indicates unsigned relation

commonMistakes: Confusing CMPSB with CMP - CMP compares register with memory, CMPSB compares two memory locations
Forgetting that CMPSB modifies SI and DI - they point past the compared bytes after execution
Not setting ES properly - CMPSB uses ES:DI for destination, not DS:DI
Using CMPSB with wrong size operands - comparing bytes vs words requires matching instruction
Assuming CX is preserved after REPE CMPSB - CX is decremented each iteration
Forgetting CLD before string comparisons - DF must be 0 for forward comparison

examples.code:
; SCASB and SCASW Demonstration
; Shows string scanning using SCASB and SCASW

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    buffer      DB 'FIND THE CHARACTER IN THIS STRING', 0
    names       DB 'ALICEBOBCHARLIEDAVE', 0
    numbers     DW 1111h, 2222h, 3333h, 4444h, 5555h
    search_char DB 'X'
    found_msg   DB 'Found!$'
    notfound_msg DB 'Not found$'
    at_msg      DB ' at position $'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX

    ; === Part 1: Find Character with REPNE SCASB ===
    CLD
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV CX, 30              ; Search up to 30 bytes
    MOV AL, 'C'             ; Character to find
    REPNE SCASB             ; Scan while [ES:DI] != AL
    ; After REPNE SCASB:
    ;   If ZF=1: Found! DI points to 'C'
    ;   If ZF=0 and CX=0: Not found in 30 bytes
    JZ char_found
    ; Not found
    JMP part2

char_found:
    ; Found 'C' at position (30 - CX)
    ; DI now points to the found 'C'

part2:
    ; === Part 2: Find First Non-Space Character ===
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV CX, 30
    MOV AL, ' '              ; Space character
    REPNE SCASB             ; Skip spaces
    ; After: DI points to first non-space or end
    JZ space_skipped

space_skipped:
    ; CX bytes were spaces, first non-space is at DI

    ; === Part 3: Find Null Terminator ===
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV CX, 0FFFFh          ; Max search (65535 bytes)
    XOR AL, AL              ; AL = 0 (null)
    REPNE SCASB             ; Search for null
    ; After: DI points past null, CX = 0
    ; String length = 30 (original CX - CX)

    ; === Part 4: Count Occurrences of Character ===
    LEA DI, names
    MOV CX, 20              ; Length of names string
    MOV AL, 'A'             ; Count 'A's
    XOR BX, BX              ; BX = counter

count_loop:
    SCASB                     ; Compare AL with [ES:DI], update DI
    JNE not_an_a
    INC BX                  ; Found an 'A'
not_an_a:
    LOOP count_loop
    ; BX = number of 'A' characters found

    ; === Part 5: Find Word in Word Array using SCASW ===
    CLD
    LEA DI, numbers
    MOV CX, 5               ; Search 5 words
    MOV AX, 3333h            ; Word to find
    REPNE SCASW             ; Scan while [ES:DI] != AX
    ; After REPNE SCASW:
    ;   If ZF=1: Found! DI points to 3333h
    ;   If ZF=0: Not found
    JZ word_found

word_found:
    ; Found 3333h at position (5 - CX) = 2

    ; === Part 6: Search with Early Termination ===
    ; Find 'Z' in buffer (should not be found)
    LEA DI, buffer
    MOV CX, 30
    MOV AL, 'Z'
    REPNE SCASB
    ; CX will be 0 if we searched entire buffer
    ; ZF will be 0 because 'Z' is not in buffer
    JNZ not_in_buffer

not_in_buffer:
    ; 'Z' not found - CX = 0, ZF = 0

    ; === Part 7: Find Vowel in String ===
    LEA DI, names
    MOV CX, 20
    MOV AL, 'E'             ; Looking for 'E'
    REPNE SCASB
    ; When found: position = 20 - CX
    ; 'E' appears in ALICE and CHARLIE

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: SCASB compares AL with byte at [ES:DI] and sets flags, then DI is updated
SCASB is like CMP AL, [ES:DI] but doesn't modify memory, only sets flags
REPNE SCASB repeats scanning while bytes don't match and CX>0
REPNE SCASB: exits when found (ZF=1) or CX=0 (searched all)
Position of found character = original_CX - remaining_CX
Finding null terminator: scan for AL=0, DI will be past null when found
SCASB only updates DI, not SI - useful for searching in destination buffer
SCASW compares AX with word at [ES:DI], updates DI by 2
SCASW is efficient for searching arrays of 16-bit values
After REPNE SCASW: ZF=1 means word found, ZF=0 means not found (CX=0)

commonMistakes: Confusing SCASB with CMPSB - SCASB compares AL with memory, CMPSB compares two memory locations
Forgetting that SCASB uses ES:DI, not DS:SI - must set ES properly
Not initializing AL before SCASB - AL must contain the value you're searching for
Confusing REPE with REPNE for searching - REPNE finds matching value, REPE finds non-matching
Not setting DF correctly - DF=0 (CLD) for forward scanning
Forgetting that SCASB updates DI automatically - don't increment DI in the loop

examples.code:
; CLD and STD Demonstration
; Demonstrates Direction Flag control and its effect on string operations

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    source      DB 'ABCDEFGHIJ', 0
    dest_fwd    DB 20 DUP(?)
    dest_bwd    DB 20 DUP(?)
    buffer      DB 100 DUP(?)
    header1     DB '=== Forward Direction (CLD) ===', 13, 10, '$'
    header2     DB 13, 10, '=== Backward Direction (STD) ===', 13, 10, '$'
    footer      DB 13, 10, '=== Demo Complete ===', 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX

    ; === Part 1: Forward Copy with CLD ===
    ; CLD clears DF: SI and DI will INCREMENT after each string operation
    CLD                     ; DF = 0 (forward/increment)
    
    ; Set up pointers to START of both strings
    LEA SI, source          ; SI = start of source
    LEA DI, dest_fwd        ; DI = start of destination
    
    ; Count string length first
    MOV CX, 0               ; Counter
count_fwd:
    CMP BYTE PTR [SI + CX], 0
    JE count_fwd_done
    INC CX
    CMP CX, 100
    JNE count_fwd
count_fwd_done:
    
    ; Copy using LODSB/STOSB pattern (forward)
    MOV DX, CX              ; Save length
    MOV CX, DX              ; Restore count
    
copy_fwd:
    LODSB                   ; AL = [DS:SI], SI = SI + 1 (because DF=0)
    STOSB                   ; [ES:DI] = AL, DI = DI + 1 (because DF=0)
    LOOP copy_fwd
    
    ; Result: dest_fwd now contains 'ABCDEFGHIJ'
    
    ; === Part 2: Backward Copy with STD ===
    ; STD sets DF: SI and DI will DECREMENT after each string operation
    STD                     ; DF = 1 (backward/decrement)
    
    ; Set up pointers to END of strings (last valid byte)
    LEA SI, source[9]       ; SI = end of source (index 9 = 'J')
    LEA DI, dest_bwd[9]    ; DI = end of destination
    
    ; Copy 10 bytes backward
    MOV CX, 10
    
copy_bwd:
    LODSB                   ; AL = [DS:SI], SI = SI - 1 (because DF=1)
    STOSB                   ; [ES:DI] = AL, DI = DI - 1 (because DF=1)
    LOOP copy_bwd
    
    ; Reset to forward direction for safety
    CLD
    
    ; Result: dest_bwd now contains 'ABCDEFGHIJ' (same as source)
    ; Note: We copied from end to start, but stored end to start,
    ; so the byte order is preserved!
    
    ; === Part 3: Practical - Build String in Reverse ===
    CLD
    LEA DI, buffer          ; Start of buffer
    
    ; Write 'A', 'B', 'C' but we'll build it in reverse order
    ; First, fill buffer with spaces (forward)
    MOV AL, ' '
    MOV CX, 50
    REP STOSB               ; DI increments with each store
    
    ; Now use STD to write backwards from position 20
    STD
    LEA DI, buffer[19]     ; Point to position 19 (20th byte, 0-indexed)
    
    ; Write 'C', 'B', 'A' in reverse
    MOV AL, 'C'
    STOSB                   ; DI = DI - 1 = 18
    MOV AL, 'B'
    STOSB                   ; DI = DI - 1 = 17
    MOV AL, 'A'
    STOSB                   ; DI = DI - 1 = 16
    
    ; Result: buffer[17]='A', buffer[18]='B', buffer[19]='C'
    
    ; Reset to forward
    CLD
    
    ; === Part 4: Demonstrate REP with Different Directions ===
    ; Clear both destinations
    LEA DI, dest_fwd
    MOV AL, 0
    MOV CX, 20
    REP STOSB               ; Clear 20 bytes (forward with CLD)
    
    LEA DI, dest_bwd
    MOV CX, 20
    STD
    REP STOSB               ; Clear 20 bytes (backward with STD)
    CLD
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: CLD clears the direction flag, setting DF=0 so SI and DI increment after string ops
LEA SI, source loads the offset of source - SI points to first byte
LODSB with DF=0: AL = [DS:SI], then SI increments by 1
STOSB with DF=0: [ES:DI] = AL, then DI increments by 1
STD sets the direction flag, DF=1 so SI and DI decrement after string ops
When DF=1, string operations work backward from high addresses to low
source[9] is the 10th byte (0-indexed) = 'J' - perfect for backward processing
LODSB with DF=1: AL = [DS:SI], then SI decrements by 1
STOSB with DF=1: [ES:DI] = AL, then DI decrements by 1
REP STOSB with CLD fills buffer from low to high addresses
With STD, DI starts at buffer[19] and decrements as we write
After writing CBA with STD at buffer[19], positions 17-19 contain ABC

commonMistakes: Forgetting to set DF before string operations - wrong DF means wrong direction
Confusing CLD and STD - CLD clears (0=forward), STD sets (1=backward)
Not setting ES for STOSB when using STD - ES:DI is used for destination
Starting backward copy at wrong address - must start at the END for backward
Forgetting to reset DF to 0 (CLD) after STD operations
Assuming DF state persists - always set DF explicitly before string operations

examples.code:
; Complete String Processing Demonstration
; Combines multiple string operations for practical text processing

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    input_str   DB 'hello world from assembly!', 0
    output_str  DB 50 DUP(?)
    search_str  DB 'assembly programming is fun!', 0
    target_char DB 'a'
    result_msg  DB 'Result: $'
    found_msg   DB 'Found at position $'
    notfound_msg DB 'Character not found', 13, 10, '$'
    equal_msg   DB 'Strings are EQUAL', 13, 10, '$'
    nequal_msg  DB 'Strings are NOT equal', 13, 10, '$'
    upper_msg   DB 13, 10, 'Uppercase: $'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    MOV ES, AX

    ; === Part 1: Copy and Uppercase String ===
    ; Combines LODSB (read) + processing + STOSB (write)
    CLD
    LEA SI, input_str         ; Source
    LEA DI, output_str        ; Destination
    
uppercase_loop:
    LODSB                       ; AL = [DS:SI], SI = SI + 1
    CMP AL, 0                   ; Check for null terminator
    JE uppercase_done
    
    ; Convert lowercase to uppercase (ASCII: 'a'=61h, 'z'=7Ah)
    CMP AL, 'a'
    JB not_lowercase
    CMP AL, 'z'
    JA not_lowercase
    SUB AL, 20H                ; Convert to uppercase
not_lowercase:
    
    STOSB                       ; Store to output, DI = DI + 1
    JMP uppercase_loop
    
uppercase_done:
    MOV AL, 0
    STOSB                       ; Null terminate
    
    ; Result: output_str now contains 'HELLO WORLD FROM ASSEMBLY!'
    
    ; === Part 2: Find Character in String (SCASB) ===
    CLD
    LEA DI, search_str         ; String to search
    MOV CX, 30                  ; Maximum length
    MOV AL, 'a'                 ; Character to find
    
    ; Save original CX for position calculation
    PUSH CX
    
    REPNE SCASB                 ; Search while [ES:DI] != AL
    ; After: ZF=1 means found, ZF=0 means not found
    ; CX has remaining count
    
    JZ char_found
    
    ; Character not found
    POP CX                      ; Clean up stack
    ; Would print 'not found' message here
    JMP part3
    
char_found:
    POP CX                      ; Get original CX
    ; Position = original_CX - remaining_CX
    MOV AX, CX
    SUB AX, CX                  ; AX = original_CX - remaining_CX
    ; AX now holds position
    
    ; === Part 3: String Comparison (CMPSB) ===
part3:
    ; Compare 'hello' with 'hello' (should be equal)
    LEA SI, input_str          ; First string
    LEA DI, output_str         ; Our uppercase version (different!)
    MOV CX, 5                  ; Compare first 5 characters
    
    REPE CMPSB                  ; Compare while equal (ZF=1)
    ; After: CX=0 and ZF=1 means equal, otherwise not equal
    
    JZ strings_equal
    
    ; Not equal - 'hello' != 'HELLO' (case differs)
    ; Print 'not equal' message
    JMP part4
    
strings_equal:
    ; This won't execute since hello != HELLO
    
    ; === Part 4: Find and Replace ===
    ; Find '!' and replace with '.'
part4:
    CLD
    LEA DI, input_str           ; Search in input_str
    MOV CX, 30                  ; Max length
    MOV AL, '!'                 ; Find this
    
search_exclaim:
    REPNE SCASB                 ; Search for '!'
    JZ found_exclaim
    JMP part5                   ; Not found
    
found_exclaim:
    ; DI points to '!' - replace it
    MOV AL, '.'                 ; Replace with '.'
    STOSB                       ; Store replacement (DI already at position)
    
    ; === Part 5: Count Vowels in String ===
part5:
    CLD
    LEA SI, input_str           ; Count vowels in input_str
    MOV CX, 30                  ; Max length
    XOR DX, DX                  ; DX = vowel counter
    
vowel_loop:
    LODSB                       ; Get character
    CMP AL, 0                   ; Null terminator?
    JE vowel_done
    
    ; Check if vowel (A, E, I, O, U - uppercase or lowercase)
    CMP AL, 'A'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'E'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'I'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'O'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'U'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'a'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'e'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'i'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'o'
    JE is_vowel
    CMP AL, 'u'
    JE is_vowel
    JMP not_vowel
    
is_vowel:
    INC DX
not_vowel:
    LOOP vowel_loop
    
vowel_done:
    ; DX = vowel count (should be 6: hello world - 'o', 'o', 'o')
    
    ; === Part 6: Reverse Copy with Transformation ===
    ; Copy input_str to output_str in reverse, converting to uppercase
    CLD
    LEA SI, input_str           ; Source (forward)
    LEA DI, output_str          ; Destination (we'll write backward)
    
    ; First, find length
    MOV CX, 0
len_loop:
    CMP BYTE PTR [SI + CX], 0
    JE len_done
    INC CX
    CMP CX, 100
    JNE len_loop
len_done:
    
    ; Now copy in reverse
    ; SI starts at input_str, will increment
    ; DI starts at output_str + length - 1, will decrement
    PUSH CX                     ; Save length
    
    MOV DI, SI                 ; DI = SI (start of source)
    ADD DI, CX                 ; DI = end of source
    DEC DI                      ; DI = last valid byte
    
    LEA SI, output_str          ; We'll write to output_str
    ADD SI, CX
    DEC SI                      ; SI = end of output position
    
    STD                         ; Set direction = backward
    
rev_copy:
    LODSB                       ; AL = [DS:SI], SI = SI - 1
    ; AL has character from input_str
    
    ; Convert to uppercase if lowercase
    CMP AL, 'a'
    JB store_char
    CMP AL, 'z'
    JA store_char
    SUB AL, 20H
store_char:
    
    MOV [DI], AL                ; Store to output (DI was set to output_str+len-1)
    DEC DI                      ; Move backward in destination
    
    LOOP rev_copy
    
    CLD                         ; Reset direction
    
    POP CX
    ; Result: output_str contains reversed, uppercased string
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: LODSB loads byte into AL and increments SI (forward direction)
Check for null terminator to stop processing string
ASCII range for lowercase letters: 'a'=61h(97) to 'z'=7Ah(122)
SUB AL, 20H converts lowercase to uppercase (adds 32 decimal)
STOSB stores AL to [ES:DI] and increments DI (forward)
SCASB compares AL with [ES:DI] - perfect for searching in strings
REPNE SCASB searches until AL matches or CX=0
CMPSB compares [DS:SI] with [ES:DI] and sets flags
REPE CMPSB repeats while bytes are equal - good for equality checks
After finding '!', STOSB overwrites it with '.' since DI is already there
Vowel counting combines LODSB with conditional checks in a loop
STD sets direction flag for backward processing - SI and DI will decrement
LODSB with DF=1: AL = [DS:SI], SI decrements
Writing to [DI] directly when DI points to our output buffer position

commonMistakes: Forgetting to null-terminate output strings after building them
Not checking for null terminator in loops that should stop at end of string
Confusing LODSB (loads TO AL) with STOSB (stores FROM AL)
Forgetting to set ES before using STOSB and SCASB
Not resetting DF to 0 (CLD) after using STD
Using REPE when REPNE is needed for searching - REPE finds matches, REPNE finds non-matches

examples.code:
; Locality Principles Demonstration
; Demonstrates spatial and temporal locality in array access patterns

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Array with 16 elements (each 2 bytes = word)
    array DW 16 DUP(0)
    result_msg DB 'Sum completed$'
    
.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; Initialize array with values 1, 2, 3, ... 16
    MOV CX, 16              ; Counter for initialization
    MOV BX, 0              ; Index
init_loop:
    MOV AX, CX
    MOV [array + BX], AX   ; array[i] = i+1
    ADD BX, 2              ; Next word (2 bytes)
    LOOP init_loop
    
    ; === GOOD LOCALITY: Stride-1 Sequential Access ===
    ; This loop accesses array elements sequentially
    ; Each access is near the previous one (spatial locality)
    ; Same array elements accessed repeatedly (temporal locality)
    MOV CX, 16              ; 16 iterations
    MOV BX, 0              ; Index = 0
    XOR AX, AX              ; AX = 0 (accumulator)
    
sum_stride1:
    ADD AX, [array + BX]   ; Load and add - cache will help!
    ADD BX, 2              ; Move to next element (stride-1)
    LOOP sum_stride1
    
    ; AX now contains 1+2+3+...+16 = 136
    ; This runs FAST because cache loads adjacent elements
    
    ; === BAD LOCALITY: Stride-N Sequential Access ===
    ; This loop accesses every 4th element
    ; Poor spatial locality - cache can't help much
    MOV CX, 4               ; Only 4 iterations
    MOV BX, 0              ; Index = 0
    XOR DX, DX              ; DX = 0 (accumulator)
    
sum_stride4:
    ADD DX, [array + BX]   ; Load element at stride-4
    ADD BX, 8              ; Skip 4 elements (8 bytes = 4 words)
    LOOP sum_stride4
    
    ; DX now contains array[0] + array[4] + array[8] + array[12]
    ; This runs SLOWER because each cache line serves fewer elements
    
    ; === VERY BAD LOCALITY: Random Access ===
    ; No spatial locality at all - worst case
    MOV CX, 4
    MOV BX, 0
sum_random:
    CMP BX, 0
    JE use_index0
    CMP BX, 2
    JE use_index7
    CMP BX, 4
    JE use_index3
    CMP BX, 6
    JE use_index12
    JMP done_random
    
use_index0:
    MOV SI, 0
    JMP do_add
use_index7:
    MOV SI, 14             ; Element 7 (14 bytes offset)
    JMP do_add
use_index3:
    MOV SI, 6              ; Element 3 (6 bytes offset)
    JMP do_add
use_index12:
    MOV SI, 24             ; Element 12 (24 bytes offset)
    
do_add:
    ADD DX, [array + SI] ; Random access - no cache benefit
    ADD BX, 2
    LOOP sum_random
    
done_random:
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Stride-1 access: BX increments by 2 (word size) each iteration - best locality
ADD BX, 2 maintains stride-1 - CPU cache prefetches adjacent memory
Stride-4: only 1 in 4 cache bytes used - cache efficiency drops to 25%
ADD BX, 8 skips 4 elements (8 bytes) - stride of 4 words
Random access has essentially zero spatial locality - each access is independent
Temporal locality: same loop body executed multiple times - instructions cached

commonMistakes: Assuming all loops have good locality - stride matters significantly
Not considering cache line size when choosing data structures
Random access patterns (like linked lists) have terrible locality
Accessing large arrays beyond cache size causes constant evictions
Not aligning data to cache boundaries when beneficial

examples.code:
; Memory Hierarchy Demonstration
; Simulates the concept of memory levels and caching

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Level 4: Main memory (large, slow) - 64 bytes
    main_memory DB 64 DUP(0FFh)
    
    ; Level 3: Cache (smaller, faster) - simulated as registers
    ; In real hardware, L1/L2 cache is SRAM on-chip
    cache_valid DB 0          ; Cache has valid data?
    cache_data DB 0           ; Cached value
    cache_tag DB 0FFh         ; Tag to identify cached address
    
    ; Level 2: Simulated L1 cache as variables
    l1_valid DB 0
    l1_data DB 0
    l1_tag DB 0FFh
    
    ; Data request tracking
    l1_hits DB 0
    l2_hits DB 0
    memory_accesses DB 0
    
.CODE
; Simulate cache lookup - returns value in AL if hit, sets CF if miss
CHECK_L1 PROC
    CMP l1_valid, 1          ; Is L1 cache valid?
    JNE l1_miss
    ; L1 hit!
    MOV AL, l1_data
    INC l1_hits
    CLC                      ; Clear carry = hit
    RET
    
l1_miss:
    STC                      ; Set carry = miss
    RET
CHECK_L1 ENDP

CHECK_L2 PROC
    CMP l2_valid, 1
    JNE l2_miss
    ; L2 hit!
    MOV AL, l2_data
    INC l2_hits
    CLC
    RET
    
l2_miss:
    STC
    RET
CHECK_L2 ENDP

MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Simulate Memory Hierarchy Access ===
    
    ; First access - cache miss, load from memory
    MOV BX, 10h              ; Address to access
    
    ; Check L1 cache
    CALL CHECK_L1
    JNC got_from_l1          ; If no carry, L1 hit
    
    ; Check L2 cache
    CALL CHECK_L2
    JNC got_from_l2          ; If no carry, L2 hit
    
    ; Miss in all caches - access main memory
    MOV AL, [main_memory + BX]
    INC memory_accesses
    
    ; Load into L2, then L1 (simulating cache hierarchy)
    MOV l2_data, AL
    MOV l2_tag, BL
    MOV l2_valid, 1
    
    MOV l1_data, AL
    MOV l1_tag, BL
    MOV l1_valid, 1
    
    JMP access_done
    
got_from_l2:
    ; L2 hit - promote data to L1
    MOV l1_data, AL
    MOV l1_tag, BL
    MOV l1_valid, 1
    JMP access_done
    
got_from_l1:
    ; L1 hit - data already where needed
    ; No promotion needed
    
access_done:
    
    ; === Demonstrate Cache Block Concept ===
    ; When a cache miss occurs, a whole BLOCK is loaded
    ; Block size > 1 means spatial locality helps
    
    ; Simulate loading a 4-byte block starting at address 20h
    MOV CX, 4               ; Block size = 4 bytes
    MOV SI, 20h              ; Starting address
    
load_block:
    ; In real hardware: entire cache line loaded at once
    ; Here we simulate the loading process
    MOV AL, [main_memory + SI]
    ; (In real CPU: cache controller does this automatically)
    INC SI
    LOOP load_block
    
    ; Result: next 4 accesses at addresses 20h-23h will hit cache!
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: L1 cache: smallest (32-64KB), fastest (~4 cycles) - checked first
Cache uses tag to identify which memory address is stored
L2 cache: larger (256KB-8MB), slower (~10 cycles) - checked if L1 misses
Main memory: largest (GB), slowest (~100 cycles) - only accessed on cache miss
Block loading: entire cache line fetched at once, exploiting spatial locality
After block load, next 4 bytes at 20h-23h will hit in cache (if block size = 4)

commonMistakes: Thinking cache size doesn't matter - larger cache = more data cached = fewer misses
Ignoring block size - larger blocks mean better spatial locality but higher miss penalty
Assuming all memory access is equal - registers (1 cycle) vs DRAM (100 cycles) is 100x difference
Not considering that cache has fixed size - working set too large causes constant evictions
Confusing cache level responsibilities - L1 managed by hardware, OS manages main memory

examples.code:
; Cache Hits and Misses Demonstration
; Simulates cache behavior with different access patterns

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Simulated cache with 4 lines, each holding 2 bytes
    ; For direct-mapped cache: 4 sets, 1 line each
    cache_valid DB 0, 0, 0, 0     ; Valid bits for 4 lines
    cache_tag DB 0, 0, 0, 0       ; Tag for each line
    cache_data DW 0, 0, 0, 0      ; Data (word per line)
    
    ; Statistics
    hits DW 0
    misses DW 0
    
    ; Messages
    hit_msg DB 'Cache HIT$'
    miss_msg DB 'Cache MISS$'

.CODE
; Simulate cache access - returns AX with data, sets BX=0 hit, BX=1 miss
ACCESS_CACHE PROC
    ; Address in DX, expected: tag in upper bits, set index in middle, offset in lower
    ; For our 4-line cache: set = bits [1:0] of address, offset = bit [0] (word within block)
    
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    
    ; Extract set index from address (bits 0-1 for 4 sets)
    MOV AX, DX
    AND AX, 03h              ; AX = set index (0-3)
    
    ; Check if valid and tag matches
    MOV CX, AX               ; CX = set index
    CMP cache_valid[CX], 1   ; Is line valid?
    JNE cache_miss
    
    MOV AX, cache_tag[CX]    ; Get stored tag
    MOV DX, SP
    MOV DX, [DX+2]          ; Get original address (rough approximation)
    SHR DX, 2               ; Shift to get tag bits
    AND DX, 0FFh            ; Limit tag size
    
    CMP AX, DX               ; Tags match?
    JNE cache_miss
    
    ; HIT!
    MOV AX, cache_data[CX] ; Get data
    INC hits                ; Update statistics
    MOV BX, 0               ; Return 0 = hit
    JMP access_done
    
cached_miss:
    ; MISS - load from memory (simulated)
    MOV BX, 1               ; Return 1 = miss
    INC misses
    
    ; Simulate eviction if cache is full (always evict for direct-mapped)
    MOV cache_valid[CX], 1  ; Mark as valid
    MOV cache_tag[CX], DL   ; Store new tag
    ; In real system, would load full block from memory
    MOV cache_data[CX], DX ; Store data
    
access_done:
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
ACCESS_CACHE ENDP

MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; === Pattern 1: Consecutive Accesses (Good Locality) ===
    ; Access addresses 0, 1, 2, 3 - should have good hit rate after first miss
    
    MOV DX, 0            ; Address 0
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    MOV DX, 1            ; Address 1 - same block as 0, HIT!
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    MOV DX, 2            ; Address 2 - same block, HIT!
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    MOV DX, 3            ; Address 3 - same block, HIT!
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    ; === Pattern 2: Conflict Miss Pattern ===
    ; In direct-mapped cache, addresses that map to same set conflict
    ; Address 0 and 4 map to set 0 (if block size = 2)
    
    MOV DX, 0            ; Access set 0
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    MOV DX, 4            ; Address 4 also maps to set 0 - CONFLICT MISS!
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    MOV DX, 0            ; Back to address 0 - CONFLICT MISS again!
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    MOV DX, 4            ; Address 4 - CONFLICT MISS again!
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    
    ; === Pattern 3: Capacity Miss Pattern ===
    ; Working set larger than cache causes capacity misses
    ; (Simulated - our simple cache can't really show this)
    
    MOV CX, 8               ; Try to access 8 different blocks
    MOV DX, 0
capacity_loop:
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE
    ADD DX, 4               ; Each access different block
    LOOP capacity_loop
    
    ; Result: First 4 misses (cold start), then constant misses
    ; because our cache has only 4 lines and 8 blocks = capacity miss
    
    ; === Demonstrate Three Types of Misses ===
    
    ; 1. COMPULSORY MISS: First access to any address
