    MOV DX, 10h             ; Never accessed before
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE       ; MUST miss - cache empty
    
    ; 2. CONFLICT MISS: Different addresses map to same set
    ; (Shown above with addresses 0, 4 pattern)
    
    ; 3. CAPACITY MISS: Cache too small for working set
    ; (Simulated above with 8 blocks in 4-line cache)
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Cache hit: valid bit = 1 AND tag matches - data returned from cache
Cache miss: either line invalid or tag mismatch - must fetch from memory
Victim block: the block being evicted to make room for new block
Compulsory miss: first access to address - cache is cold, no choice but to miss
Conflict miss: addresses 0 and 4 map to same cache set - direct-mapped limitation
Capacity miss: 8 blocks for 4-line cache - not enough capacity, constant evictions

commonMistakes: Confusing compulsory and conflict misses - compulsory happens once per address, conflict repeats
Thinking more cache always helps - but if working set grows faster, no improvement
Ignoring replacement policy impact - LRU is better than random but costs more hardware
Not understanding that direct-mapped has more conflicts than set-associative
Thinking write policy affects misses - write-through/write-back only affect writes, not hit/miss rate

examples.code:
; Direct-Mapped Cache Demonstration
; Simulates a 4-line direct-mapped cache with 2-byte blocks

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Simulated main memory (16 bytes)
    main_memory DB 00h, 11h, 22h, 33h, 44h, 55h, 66h, 77h
                DB 88h, 99h, 0AAh, 0BBh, 0CCh, 0DDh, 0EEh, 0FFh
    
    ; Cache: 4 sets, 1 line each, 2 bytes per block
    ; For address A: set = A[1:0], block offset = A[0], tag = A[3:2]
    cache_valid DB 0, 0, 0, 0          ; Valid bit for each line
    cache_tag   DB 0, 0, 0, 0           ; Tag for each line
    cache_block DB 4 DUP(2 DUP(?))      ; 2 bytes per line
    
    ; Statistics
    hits   DW 0
    misses DW 0
    
    ; Display messages
    hit_msg   DB 'HIT!$', 13, 10, '$'
    miss_msg  DB 'MISS!$', 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
; ========================================
; CHECK_CACHE: Check if address is in cache
; Input: DL = address (0-15)
; Output: AL = data if hit, sets ZF=1 for hit, ZF=0 for miss
; ========================================
CHECK_CACHE PROC
    PUSH BX
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    
    ; Extract set index (bits 0-1 for 4 sets)
    MOV AL, DL
    AND AL, 03h              ; AL = set index (0-3)
    MOV BL, AL               ; BL = set index
    XOR BH, BH               ; BX = set index for addressing
    
    ; Extract tag (bits 3-2)
    MOV CL, DL
    SHR CL, 2                ; CL = tag
    
    ; Check if valid and tag matches
    CMP cache_valid[BX], 1   ; Is line valid?
    JNE cache_miss_proc
    
    CMP cache_tag[BX], CL    ; Does tag match?
    JNE cache_miss_proc
    
    ; HIT! Return data from cache
    MOV AL, cache_block[BX*2]
    INC hits
    POP DX
    POP CX
    POP BX
    RET
    
cache_miss_proc:
    ; MISS - return 0 (actual data would be loaded from memory)
    XOR AL, AL
    INC misses
    POP DX
    POP CX
    POP BX
    RET
CHECK_CACHE ENDP

; ========================================
; LOAD_BLOCK: Load a block from memory into cache
; Input: DL = address
; ========================================
LOAD_BLOCK PROC
    PUSH BX
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    PUSH SI
    PUSH DI
    
    ; Extract set index
    MOV AL, DL
    AND AL, 03h
    MOV BL, AL
    XOR BH, BH
    
    ; Extract tag
    MOV CL, DL
    SHR CL, 2
    
    ; Mark line as valid and store tag
    MOV cache_valid[BX], 1
    MOV cache_tag[BX], CL
    
    ; Load 2-byte block from memory (block aligned to even address)
    MOV SI, DX
    AND SI, 0FEh             ; Block start address (even)
    
    ; Copy from memory to cache
    MOV DI, BX
    SHL DI, 1                ; DI = cache block offset (BX * 2)
    
    MOV CX, 2                ; 2 bytes per block
load_loop:
    MOV AL, [main_memory + SI]
    MOV cache_block[DI], AL
    INC SI
    INC DI
    LOOP load_loop
    
    POP DI
    POP SI
    POP DX
    POP CX
    POP BX
    RET
LOAD_BLOCK ENDP

MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 1: Sequential Access (Good Locality)
    ; Addresses 0, 1, 2, 3 all map to DIFFERENT sets
    ; First access misses, subsequent hit within same block
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Access address 0 (set 0, tag 0)
    MOV DL, 0
    CALL CHECK_CACHE         ; MISS - cache empty
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK          ; Load block 0-1
    
    MOV DL, 1                ; Address 1 (same block as 0)
    CALL CHECK_CACHE         ; Should HIT
    
    MOV DL, 4                ; Address 4 (set 0, tag 1) - CONFLICT!
    CALL CHECK_CACHE         ; MISS - evicts set 0
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK          ; Load block 4-5
    
    MOV DL, 0                ; Address 0 (set 0, tag 0) - CONFLICT!
    CALL CHECK_CACHE         ; MISS - evicts set 0
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 2: Demonstrate Thrashing
    ; Alternating between addresses that map to same set
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Addresses 0 and 4 BOTH map to set 0!
    ; This causes constant eviction and reload = THRASHING
    
    MOV DL, 0
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK          ; Load address 0 (block 0-1)
    
    MOV DL, 4                ; Address 4 maps to set 0
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK          ; Evicts block 0, loads block 4-5
    
    MOV DL, 0                ; Back to address 0 - MISS again!
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK          ; Evicts block 4, reloads block 0
    
    MOV DL, 4                ; Address 4 - MISS again!
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK          ; Evicts block 0, reloads block 4
    
    ; This alternating pattern causes 100% miss rate!
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 3: Addresses and Their Cache Mapping
    ; ========================================
    ; Address | Binary | Set | Tag | Block Offset
    ; --------|--------|-----|-----|------------
    ;    0    | 0000   |  0  |  0  |     0
    ;    1    | 0001   |  0  |  0  |     1
    ;    2    | 0010   |  1  |  0  |     0
    ;    3    | 0011   |  1  |  0  |     1
    ;    4    | 0100   |  0  |  1  |     0  <-- CONFLICT with 0!
    ;    5    | 0101   |  0  |  1  |     1  <-- CONFLICT with 1!
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Set index = bits [1:0] of address - determines which cache line we check
Tag bits [3:2] must match for cache hit - identifies which block is stored
Cache hit requires BOTH valid bit = 1 AND tag match
Block aligned to even address - offset bit [0] selects within block
THRASHING: Addresses 0 and 4 map to same set, causing constant eviction

commonMistakes: Thinking set index and tag are the same - set selects WHERE to look, tag verifies WHAT is there
Ignoring block offset - offset determines which byte within the block to return
Not understanding thrashing - direct-mapped can only hold ONE block per set
Confusing block size with cache size - block size is how much we load per miss
Thinking more cache lines always helps - direct-mapped limitation is fundamental

examples.code:
; Set Associative Cache Demonstration
; Simulates a 2-way set associative cache (2 lines per set)

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Simulated main memory (16 bytes)
    main_memory DB 00h, 11h, 22h, 33h, 44h, 55h, 66h, 77h
                DB 88h, 99h, 0AAh, 0BBh, 0CCh, 0DDh, 0EEh, 0FFh
    
    ; Cache: 2 sets, 2 lines per set (2-way associative)
    ; Each line: valid(1) + tag(1) + data(2) = 4 bytes
    ; Layout: [valid0, tag0, data0_0, data0_1] for way 0
    ;         [valid1, tag1, data1_0, data1_1] for way 1
    cache_valid0 DB 0, 0          ; Valid bits for way 0 (sets 0-1)
    cache_tag0   DB 0, 0          ; Tags for way 0
    cache_data0   DB 4 DUP(?)      ; Data for way 0
    
    cache_valid1 DB 0, 0          ; Valid bits for way 1 (sets 0-1)
    cache_tag1   DB 0, 0          ; Tags for way 1
    cache_data1   DB 4 DUP(?)      ; Data for way 1
    
    ; LRU tracking: which way was used most recently (0 or 1)
    lru          DB 0, 0           ; LRU for each set
    
    ; Statistics
    hits   DW 0
    misses DW 0

.CODE
; ========================================
; ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY: Access 2-way set associative cache
; Input: DL = address (0-15)
; Output: AL = data byte
; ========================================
ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY PROC
    PUSH BX
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    PUSH SI
    
    ; Extract set index (for 2 sets: bit 1)
    MOV AL, DL
    SHR AL, 1
    AND AL, 01h              ; AL = set index (0-1)
    MOV BL, AL               ; BL = set index
    
    ; Extract tag (bits 3-2)
    MOV CL, DL
    SHR CL, 2                ; CL = tag
    
    ; Check WAY 0
    CMP cache_valid0[BX], 1
    JNE check_way1
    CMP cache_tag0[BX], CL
    JNE check_way1
    
    ; WAY 0 HIT!
    ; Data at offset 0 of block (if even address) or offset 1 (if odd)
    MOV AL, DL
    AND AL, 01h              ; AL = 0 for even, 1 for odd
    XOR AH, AH
    MOV SI, AX
    MOV AL, cache_data0[BX*2 + SI]
    INC hits
    MOV lru[BX], 1           ; Mark way 1 as most recently used
    JMP access_done_2way
    
check_way1:
    ; Check WAY 1
    CMP cache_valid1[BX], 1
    JNE cache_miss_2way
    CMP cache_tag1[BX], CL
    JNE cache_miss_2way
    
    ; WAY 1 HIT!
    MOV AL, DL
    AND AL, 01h
    XOR AH, AH
    MOV SI, AX
    MOV AL, cache_data1[BX*2 + SI]
    INC hits
    MOV lru[BX], 0           ; Mark way 0 as most recently used
    JMP access_done_2way
    
cache_miss_2way:
    ; MISS - need to load from memory
    INC misses
    
    ; Determine which way to evict (use LRU policy)
    CMP lru[BX], 0
    JE evict_way0
    
    ; Evict WAY 1 and load new block
    MOV cache_valid1[BX], 1
    MOV cache_tag1[BX], CL
    
    ; Load 2-byte block from memory
    MOV SI, DX
    AND SI, 0FEh             ; Block start (aligned)
    MOV AL, [main_memory + SI]
    MOV cache_data1[BX*2], AL
    MOV AL, [main_memory + SI + 1]
    MOV cache_data1[BX*2 + 1], AL
    
    MOV lru[BX], 0           ; Way 0 is now more recently used
    JMP access_done_2way
    
evict_way0:
    ; Evict WAY 0 and load new block
    MOV cache_valid0[BX], 1
    MOV cache_tag0[BX], CL
    
    ; Load 2-byte block from memory
    MOV SI, DX
    AND SI, 0FEh
    MOV AL, [main_memory + SI]
    MOV cache_data0[BX*2], AL
    MOV AL, [main_memory + SI + 1]
    MOV cache_data0[BX*2 + 1], AL
    
    MOV lru[BX], 1           ; Way 1 is now more recently used
    
access_done_2way:
    POP SI
    POP DX
    POP CX
    POP BX
    RET
ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY ENDP

MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 1: Addresses that conflict in direct-mapped
    ; Now they can coexist in different ways!
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Address 0: set 0, tag 0
    MOV DL, 0
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY   ; MISS
    
    ; Address 4: set 0, tag 1 (conflicts with 0 in direct-mapped!)
    ; But in 2-way: goes to WAY 1, no conflict!
    MOV DL, 4
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY   ; MISS (cold start for way 1)
    
    ; Address 0 again - STILL IN WAY 0!
    MOV DL, 0
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY   ; HIT! (avoids thrashing)
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 2: Demonstrate LRU Replacement
    ; When both ways full, LRU determines eviction
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Set 0 currently has: way0=block0, way1=block1
    ; Access block 2 (also set 0, tag 1)
    MOV DL, 8                ; Address 8: set 0, tag 2
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY   ; MISS - must evict one way
    ; LRU says evict way 0 (least recently used)
    
    ; Now access evicted block 0 - must reload
    MOV DL, 0
    CALL ACCESS_CACHE_2WAY   ; MISS - block 0 was evicted
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 3: Compare Direct-Mapped vs 2-Way
    ; ========================================
    ; Direct-mapped with addresses 0, 4, 0, 4, 0, 4...
    ; Miss rate: 100% (alternating conflict)
    ;
    ; 2-way associative with same pattern:
    ; Way 0: block 0
    ; Way 1: block 1 (first address 4)
    ; Then addresses 0, 4 HIT!
    ; Only address 8 causes eviction
    ; Miss rate: ~33% (much better!)
    
    ; ========================================
    ; Address Mapping Summary
    ; ========================================
    ; Address | Binary | Set | Tag | Block
    ; --------|--------|-----|-----|------
    ;    0    | 0000   |  0  |  0  | 0-1
    ;    4    | 0100   |  0  |  1  | 4-5  <-- same set, diff tag
    ;    8    | 1000   |  0  |  2  | 8-9  <-- same set, diff tag
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: 2-way: check both ways in parallel, return data from matching way
LRU: evict the way that hasn't been used recently
Fully associative would have 1 set with all lines - no set index bits
2-way allows addresses 0 and 4 (same set, different tags) to coexist

commonMistakes: Confusing 'ways' with 'sets' - 2-way means 2 lines PER SET, not 2 total lines
Thinking associativity is free - checking multiple lines costs hardware complexity and power
Ignoring LRU overhead - true LRU needs to track order of all line accesses
Not understanding fully associative - tag is entire address minus offset, very expensive
Assuming higher associativity always better - diminishing returns beyond 4-8 ways

examples.code:
; Cache Writes and Performance Demonstration
; Simulates write-through and write-back policies

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Simulated memory
    main_memory DB 16 DUP(0FFh)
    
    ; Cache with dirty bit for write-back
    cache_valid DB 1 DUP(0)
    cache_dirty DB 1 DUP(0)        ; Dirty bit - set if modified
    cache_data  DB 2 DUP(?)
    
    ; Write buffer for write-through
    write_buf_valid DB 0
    write_buf_addr  DB ?
    write_buf_data  DB ?
    
    ; Statistics
    bus_transactions DW 0         ; Counts writes to main memory
    cache_hits DW 0
    cache_misses DW 0

.CODE
; ========================================
; WRITE_THROUGH: Write data with write-through policy
; Input: DL = address, AL = data
; ========================================
WRITE_THROUGH PROC
    PUSH BX
    
    ; Check if address is in cache
    CMP cache_valid[0], 1
    JNE wt_cache_miss
    
    ; Update cache
    MOV cache_data[0], AL
    
    ; ALSO write directly to memory (immediate bus transaction)
    MOV BX, DX
    MOV [main_memory + BX], AL
    INC bus_transactions
    
    JMP wt_done
    
wt_cache_miss:
    ; With write-no-allocate: only update memory, don't load block
    MOV BX, DX
    MOV [main_memory + BX], AL
    INC bus_transactions
    
    ; With write-allocate (commented): load block, then update
    ; CALL LOAD_BLOCK
    ; MOV cache_data[0], AL
    
wt_done:
    POP BX
    RET
WRITE_THROUGH ENDP

; ========================================
; WRITE_BACK: Write data with write-back policy
; Input: DL = address, AL = data
; ========================================
WRITE_BACK PROC
    PUSH BX
    
    ; Check if address is in cache
    CMP cache_valid[0], 1
    JNE wb_cache_miss
    
    ; Update cache and mark dirty
    MOV cache_data[0], AL
    MOV cache_dirty[0], 1
    ; NO bus transaction yet!
    
    JMP wb_done
    
wb_cache_miss:
    ; Write-allocate: load block into cache, then update
    CALL LOAD_BLOCK_WB
    MOV cache_data[0], AL
    MOV cache_dirty[0], 1
    
wb_done:
    POP BX
    RET
WRITE_BACK ENDP

; ========================================
; LOAD_BLOCK_WB: Load block from memory
; Input: DL = address
; ========================================
LOAD_BLOCK_WB PROC
    PUSH BX
    PUSH DX
    
    ; Check if current block is dirty - if so, write back first!
    CMP cache_valid[0], 1
    JNE no_evict_needed
    CMP cache_dirty[0], 1
    JNE no_evict_needed
    
    ; Write dirty block back to memory before loading new one
    MOV BX, 0                ; Address (would need tag check in real impl)
    MOV AL, cache_data[0]
    MOV [main_memory + BX], AL
    INC bus_transactions
    
no_evict_needed:
    ; Load new block from memory
    POP DX
    PUSH DX
    MOV BX, DX
    AND BX, 0FEh              ; Block aligned address
    MOV cache_valid[0], 1
    MOV cache_dirty[0], 0
    MOV AL, [main_memory + BX]
    MOV cache_data[0], AL
    
    POP DX
    POP BX
    RET
LOAD_BLOCK_WB ENDP

; ========================================
; EVICT_BLOCK: Force eviction of dirty block
; ========================================
EVICT_BLOCK PROC
    CMP cache_dirty[0], 1
    JNE evict_clean
    
    ; Write dirty block to memory
    MOV BX, 0
    MOV AL, cache_data[0]
    MOV [main_memory + BX], AL
    INC bus_transactions
    MOV cache_dirty[0], 0
    
evict_clean:
    MOV cache_valid[0], 0
    RET
EVICT_BLOCK ENDP

MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 1: Write-Through vs Write-Back
    ; ========================================
    
    ; Scenario: Write to same location 5 times
    
    ; Write-Through: 5 bus transactions
    MOV cache_valid[0], 1
    MOV DL, 10h
    MOV AL, 0AAh
    CALL WRITE_THROUGH        ; Bus transaction 1
    
    MOV AL, 0BBh
    CALL WRITE_THROUGH        ; Bus transaction 2
    
    MOV AL, 0CCh
    CALL WRITE_THROUGH        ; Bus transaction 3
    
    MOV AL, 0DDh
    CALL WRITE_THROUGH        ; Bus transaction 4
    
    MOV AL, 0EEh
    CALL WRITE_THROUGH        ; Bus transaction 5
    
    ; Reset cache
    MOV cache_valid[0], 1
    MOV cache_dirty[0], 0
    
    ; Write-Back: 0 bus transactions (until eviction)
    MOV DL, 10h
    MOV AL, 0AAh
    CALL WRITE_BACK           ; No bus transaction!
    
    MOV AL, 0BBh
    CALL WRITE_BACK           ; No bus transaction!
    
    MOV AL, 0CCh
    CALL WRITE_BACK           ; No bus transaction!
    
    MOV AL, 0DDh
    CALL WRITE_BACK           ; No bus transaction!
    
    MOV AL, 0EEh
    CALL WRITE_BACK           ; No bus transaction!
    
    ; Bus transaction only happens when we EVICT the dirty block
    CALL EVICT_BLOCK          ; Bus transaction 1 (writing 0EEh)
    
    ; Result: Write-back uses FAR fewer bus transactions!
    
    ; ========================================
    ; TEST 2: Write Buffer Benefit
    ; ========================================
    ; Write buffer allows CPU to continue without waiting
    ; for memory write to complete
    
    ; Without buffer: CPU waits for each memory write
    ; With buffer: CPU continues, buffer handles memory writes async
    
    ; Simulate buffered write:
    MOV write_buf_valid, 1
    MOV write_buf_addr, 20h
    MOV write_buf_data, 55h
    ; CPU can continue immediately - doesn't wait!
    
    ; Later, buffer writes to memory (in background)
    ; This would happen while CPU does other work
    MOV BX, 20h
    MOV AL, write_buf_data
    MOV [main_memory + BX], AL
    INC bus_transactions
    MOV write_buf_valid, 0
    
    ; ========================================
    ; Performance Comparison Summary
    ; ========================================
    ; Write-Through:
    ;   + Simple to implement
    ;   + Memory always consistent with cache
    ;   - Many bus transactions (slow)
    ;
    ; Write-Back:
    ;   + Fewer bus transactions (fast)
    ;   + More complex (dirty bits, write-back on eviction)
    ;   - Memory may lag behind cache
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Write-through: every write goes to BOTH cache AND memory (immediate bus transaction)
Write-back: write only to cache, defer memory write until eviction
Dirty bit tracks whether cache line has been modified vs memory
Write buffer decouples CPU from slow memory - allows async writes
Miss penalty: extra time to fetch from lower level - write-back reduces this

commonMistakes: Thinking write policy affects hit rate - it only affects bus traffic and complexity
Confusing write-through with write-back - wt writes immediately, wb defers
Not understanding dirty bit purpose - tracks modification relative to memory
Ignoring write buffer benefit - allows CPU to continue while memory updates in background
Thinking write-no-allocate is always worse - sometimes avoiding cache pollution is worth it

examples.code:
; Memory Mountain Demonstration
; Shows how throughput varies with working set size and stride

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    ; Large array simulating main memory (256 bytes)
    ; In real systems, this would be much larger (MB-GB)
    array DB 256 DUP(0)
    
    ; Cache simulation parameters
    L1_SIZE EQU 64           ; L1 cache: 64 bytes (16 elements)
    L2_SIZE EQU 128          ; L2 cache: 128 bytes (32 elements)
    BLOCK_SIZE EQU 8         ; Cache block: 8 bytes
    
    ; Timing simulation (in reality, would use cycle counters)
    L1_HIT_CYCLES EQU 4      ; L1 hit takes 4 cycles
    L2_HIT_CYCLES EQU 12     ; L2 hit takes 12 cycles
    MEM_ACCESS_CYCLES EQU 100 ; Memory access takes 100 cycles
    
    ; Results storage
    throughput_report DB 256 DUP(?)

.CODE
; ========================================
; SIMULATE_ACCESS: Simulate memory access time
; Input: CX = stride (1, 2, 4, 8...)
;       BX = working set size (elements accessed)
; Output: AX = average cycles per access
; ========================================
SIMULATE_ACCESS PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    PUSH SI
    
    MOV AX, 0               ; Total cycles
    XOR SI, SI               ; Index = 0
    
access_loop:
    ; Determine which cache level (if any) this access hits
    
    ; Check L1: address in [0, L1_SIZE)?
    CMP SI, L1_SIZE
    JAE check_l2
    
    ; L1 HIT
    ADD AX, L1_HIT_CYCLES
    JMP next_access
    
check_l2:
    ; Check L2: address in [L1_SIZE, L2_SIZE)?
    CMP SI, L2_SIZE
    JAE memory_access
    
    ; L2 HIT (but L1 missed - this costs extra)
    ADD AX, L1_HIT_CYCLES    ; L1 miss penalty + L2 hit
    ADD AX, L2_HIT_CYCLES
    JMP next_access
    
memory_access:
    ; Full memory access
    ADD AX, L1_HIT_CYCLES    ; L1 miss penalty
    ADD AX, L2_HIT_CYCLES    ; L2 miss penalty
    ADD AX, MEM_ACCESS_CYCLES
    
next_access:
    ; Move to next element with stride
    MOV DX, CX               ; DX = stride
    SHL DX, 0                ; Stride in bytes (1 element = 1 byte)
    ADD SI, DX
    
    ; Loop until we've accessed working set
    DEC BX
    CMP BX, 0
    JNE access_loop
    
    ; Average cycles per access
    POP SI
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
SIMULATE_ACCESS ENDP

; ========================================
; DEMONSTRATE_PREFETCH: Show prefetch benefit
; Input: CX = stride
; ========================================
DEMONSTRATE_PREFETCH PROC
    PUSH CX
    PUSH DX
    
    ; Without prefetch: each access pays miss penalty
    MOV DX, CX               ; Same stride
    
    ; With hardware prefetch: next block fetched early
    ; When stride is predictable, prefetch can hide miss penalty
    
    ; Stride-1 (sequential): prefetch works well
    ; Prefetch distance = 1 cache block means we're ready
    
    ; Large stride: prefetch doesn't help much
    ; We jump over blocks without using them
    
    POP DX
    POP CX
    RET
DEMONSTRATE_PREFETCH ENDP

MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX
    
    ; ========================================
    ; THE MEMORY MOUNTAIN
    ; X-axis: Working set size (determines which cache level)
    ; Y-axis: Stride (determines spatial locality benefit)
    ; Z-axis: Throughput (higher is better)
    ; ========================================
    
    ; ========================================
    ; RIDGE 1: Working set fits in L1
    ; ========================================
    ; Small working set (< 64 bytes), any stride
    ; Performance is BEST - all hits in L1
    
    MOV CX, 1                ; Stride = 1
    MOV BX, 16                ; 16 elements = 16 bytes < L1_SIZE
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; AX = ~4 cycles/access (L1 ridge peak)
    
    MOV CX, 4                ; Stride = 4
    MOV BX, 16
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; Still ~4 cycles (fits in L1)
    
    ; ==========================================
    ; RIDGE 2: Working set fits in L2 but not L1
    ; ========================================
    ; Medium working set (64-128 bytes)
    ; L1 misses, L2 hits - performance drops
    
    MOV CX, 1                ; Stride = 1 (good spatial locality)
    MOV BX, 80                ; 80 bytes - doesn't fit in L1
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; ~16 cycles/access (L2 ridge)
    
    MOV CX, 8                ; Large stride - spatial locality wasted
    MOV BX, 80
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; Higher - cache blocks not fully utilized
    
    ; ========================================
    ; VALLEY: Working set too large for any cache
    ; ========================================
    ; Large working set (> 128 bytes)
    ; All cache levels miss - worst performance
    
    MOV CX, 1                ; Even stride-1 doesn't help
    MOV BX, 200               ; 200 bytes - exceeds L2
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; ~116 cycles/access (valley bottom)
    
    MOV CX, 4                ; Large stride = terrible spatial locality
    MOV BX, 200
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; Even worse - constant cache misses
    
    ; ========================================
    ; SLOPES: How performance falls with stride
    ; ========================================
    ; For working set = 32 elements (fits in L1):
    ; 
    ; Stride 1: 16 elements * 1 block each = 16 blocks
    ;            All blocks fit in L1 = HIGH throughput
    ;
    ; Stride 8: 4 elements * 8 blocks each = 32 blocks
    ;            32 blocks > L1 capacity = constant evictions
    ;            = LOW throughput
    
    MOV CX, 1                ; Good: uses full cache line
    MOV BX, 32
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; Peak L1 performance
    
    MOV CX, 8                ; Bad: skipping over blocks
    MOV BX, 32
    CALL SIMULATE_ACCESS     ; Reduced performance
    
    ; ========================================
    ; PREFETCH DEMONSTRATION
    ; ========================================
    ; Modern CPUs prefetch when they detect regular stride
    
    ; Sequential access (stride-1): prefetch works!
    ; CPU detects pattern and loads next block early
    
    ; Random access: prefetch can't help
    ; Unpredictable pattern - no benefit
    
    CALL DEMONSTRATE_PREFETCH
    
    ; ========================================
    ; OPTIMIZATION GUIDELINES
    ; ========================================
    ; 1. Maximize temporal locality:
    ;    - Keep heavily used data in L1
    ;    - Reuse loaded data before eviction
    ;
    ; 2. Maximize spatial locality:
    ;    - Use stride-1 access patterns
    ;    - Sequential access is optimal
    ;
    ; 3. Consider cache line size:
    ;    - Block size typically 64 bytes
    ;    - Stride-1 uses full block (8 accesses per block)
    ;    - Large stride wastes block capacity
    
    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4CH
    INT 21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: Memory mountain has ridges (working set fits cache) and valleys (working set too large)
Working set: amount of data accessed - determines which cache level is effective
Prefetch hides miss penalty by loading next block before it's needed
Stride determines spatial locality - stride-1 best, large stride wastes cache blocks
Optimization focus on inner loops where most memory access occurs

commonMistakes: Thinking throughput is constant - it varies dramatically with access pattern
Ignoring working set effect - too much data = cache thrashing = slow
Using large strides without reason - stride-1 always better for throughput
Not considering prefetch - predictable patterns get hardware acceleration
Over-optimizing inner loops unnecessarily - measure first, optimize where it matters

examples.code:
; Operating System Process Demonstration
; Shows how the OS manages program execution through interrupts

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    os_version   DB 'DOS Version 3.30$'
    process_msg  DB 'Process executing under OS supervision$'
    newline      DB 13, 10, '$'
    ; Simulated Process Control Block (PCB) fields
    pcb_eax      DW 0
    pcb_ebx      DW 0
    pcb_esp      DW 0
    pcb_eip      DW 0
    pcb_flags    DW 0

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX

    ; === Part 1: OS Controls Program Execution ===
    ; Programs don't directly control the CPU - OS mediates
    ; Every service (I/O, memory allocation) goes through the OS

    ; Display message through DOS interrupt (OS service)
    LEA DX, os_version
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h                    ; OS handles string display

    LEA DX, newline
    INT 21h

    ; Display process message
    LEA DX, process_msg
    INT 21h

    ; === Part 2: Program Termination Goes Through OS ===
    ; Exit function (AH=4Ch) is a "system call"
    ; User program requests OS to terminate it properly
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h                   ; TRAP - control transfers to OS kernel

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

; ========================================
; OS KERNEL SERVICES (Conceptual)
; These would be in the OS kernel, not the user program
; ========================================
; KERNEL_SERVICE PROC
;     ; When INT 21h is executed:
;     ; 1. CPU switches from user mode to kernel (privileged) mode
;     ; 2. CPU saves current process context (registers, flags, PC)
;     ; 3. CPU looks up interrupt vector table
;     ; 4. CPU transfers control to OS handler at vector address
;     ; 5. OS performs the requested service
;     ; 6. OS restores process context or terminates process
;     IRET                    ; Return from interrupt
; KERNEL_SERVICE ENDP

END MAIN

annotations.text: INT 21h is a TRAP - program requests OS service (display string)
OS acts as intermediary - program never talks directly to hardware
AH=4Ch is the standard DOS termination system call
Even program termination is controlled by OS, not program itself
INT instruction triggers exception vector lookup - control goes to OS
OS kernel runs in privileged mode - can access hardware directly

commonMistakes: Thinking program has direct control of hardware - it only requests OS services
Confusing INT instruction with regular CALL - INT triggers exception handling
Not understanding that even 'simple' I/O goes through OS kernel
Forgetting that program termination is a service request, not direct exit
Thinking user programs run at same privilege level as OS kernel

examples.code:
; Exception Handler Demonstration
; Shows how exceptions occur and how handlers process them

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    divide_err_msg   DB 'Divide Error Exception!$'
    overflow_msg     DB 'Overflow Exception!$'
    normal_msg       DB 'Normal operation completed$'
    newline           DB 13, 10, '$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX

    ; === Part 1: Divide Error Exception (INT 0) ===
    ; DIV by zero causes exception - CPU detects and transfers to handler

    MOV AX, 100
    MOV BL, 0               ; BL = 0 - will cause divide error

    ; Before DIV, check for zero divisor
    CMP BL, 0
    JE divisor_is_zero       ; Avoid the exception!

    DIV BL                   ; AX / BL -> AL=quotient, AH=remainder
    JMP continue1

divisor_is_zero:
    LEA DX, divide_err_msg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h
    JMP after_divide

continue1:
    LEA DX, normal_msg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h

after_divide:

    ; === Part 2: Arithmetic Overflow Exception (INT 4) ===
    ; INTO checks overflow flag - if OF=1, transfers to handler

    MOV AX, 32767            ; AX = +32767 (max signed word)
    ADD AX, 1                ; AX = -32768 (overflow! OF=1)

    INTO                     ; If OF=1, INT 4 occurs
    ; If OF=0, INTO is a no-op and we continue here

    ; Continue with normal flow
    LEA DX, newline
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h

    ; === Part 3: Software-Triggered Exception (INT 3) ===
    ; INT 3 is breakpoint - used by debuggers

    INT 3                     ; Breakpoint exception - transfer to debug handler
    ; Program stops here if running in debugger

    ; === Exception Handler Conceptual Code ===
    ; EXCEPTION_HANDLER PROC
    ;     ; CPU has pushed: error code, return address, flags, CS
    ;     PUSH EBP
    ;     MOV EBP, ESP
    ;
    ;     ; Determine exception type from vector number
    ;     MOV EAX, [EBP+8]     ; Get vector number
    ;     CMP EAX, 0           ; Divide error?
    ;     JE HANDLE_DIVIDE
    ;     CMP EAX, 4           ; Overflow?
    ;     JE HANDLE_OVERFLOW
    ;
    ; HANDLE_DIVIDE:
    ;     ; Send signal to process or recover
    ;     JMP COMMON_HANDLER
    ;
    ; HANDLE_OVERFLOW:
    ;     ; Handle overflow condition
    ;     JMP COMMON_HANDLER
    ;
    ; COMMON_HANDLER:
    ;     ; Return control to next instruction (IRET)
    ;     POP EBP
    ;     IRET                 ; Return to interrupted program
    ; EXCEPTION_HANDLER ENDP

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: DIV with divisor=0 triggers divide error exception (INT 0)
CPU detects exception BEFORE executing the instruction that would divide by zero
Compare to zero BEFORE DIV prevents exception by checking first
ADD sets overflow flag when signed result doesn't fit in destination
INTO checks OF - if set, generates INT 4 (overflow exception handler)
INT 3 is breakpoint trap - debuggers use it to pause program execution
Exception handler receives control - determines type and responds
IRET restores saved state and returns control to interrupted program

commonMistakes: Not checking divisor before DIV - divide-by-zero causes exception
Forgetting that INTO checks OF but doesn't modify it
Confusing INT 3 (breakpoint) with INT 21h (DOS service) - different handlers
Thinking exceptions can be ignored - CPU must respond to hardware events
Not understanding that exception handlers run in kernel (privileged) mode

examples.code:
; Exception Classes Demonstration
; Shows the four classes of exceptions: Interrupt, Trap, Fault, Abort

.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H

.DATA
    int_msg     DB 'INTERRUPT: Async hardware event detected$'
    trap_msg    DB 'TRAP: Intentional service request (system call)$'
    fault_msg   DB 'FAULT: Recoverable error - will retry instruction$'
    abort_msg   DB 'ABORT: Unrecoverable error - program terminated$'
    newline     DB 13, 10, '$'
    syscall_msg DB 'Performing system call: INT 21h$'

.CODE
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, @DATA
    MOV DS, AX

    ; ========================================
    ; CLASS 1: INTERRUPT (Asynchronous)
    ; ========================================
    ; Caused by hardware events outside CPU control
    ; Examples: keyboard input, mouse movement, timer tick
    ; NOT caused by executing instructions
    ; Handler returns to NEXT instruction (Inext)

    LEA DX, int_msg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h                     ; DOS service is itself an interrupt
    ; In real scenario, hardware timer would trigger INT 08h
    ; independent of program execution

    LEA DX, newline
    INT 21h

    ; ========================================
    ; CLASS 2: TRAP (Intentional - System Calls)
    ; ========================================
    ; Program explicitly requests OS service
    ; INT instruction is the mechanism
    ; Handler returns to NEXT instruction (Inext)
    ; "System calls" provide procedure-like interface to OS


    LEA DX, syscall_msg
    INT 21h

    LEA DX, trap_msg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h                     ; TRAP: INT 21h is intentional service request

    ; Example: Function 01h (keyboard read) is a TRAP
    ; Program executes INT 21h to request OS read keyboard
    ; OS reads character, returns to next instruction

    LEA DX, newline
    INT 21h

    ; ========================================
    ; CLASS 3: FAULT (Possibly Recoverable)
    ; ========================================
    ; Caused by error condition in current instruction
    ; If handler can fix: retry CURRENT instruction (Icurr)
    ; If cannot fix: terminate program
    ; Example: Page fault - handler loads page from disk, retries

    LEA DX, fault_msg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h

    ; Simulate page fault handling conceptually:
    ; 1. Program accesses memory location
    ; 2. MMU detects page not in physical memory
    ; 3. Page fault exception (INT 14 or similar)
    ; 4. Handler loads page from disk
    ; 5. Handler updates page table
    ; 6. IRET returns to SAME instruction that caused fault
    ; 7. Program re-executes instruction - now it works

    LEA DX, newline
    INT 21h

    ; ========================================
    ; CLASS 4: ABORT (Unrecoverable)
    ; ========================================
    ; Hardware errors or corrupted memory
    ; Handler does NOT return control to failing program
    ; Simply terminates process - no recovery possible

    LEA DX, abort_msg
    MOV AH, 09h
    INT 21h

    ; ========================================
    ; SUMMARY: Exception Class Behavior
    ; ========================================
    ;
    ; Class      | Cause              | Returns to      | Recovery
    ; -----------+--------------------+-----------------+----------
    ; INTERRUPT   | Hardware (async)   | Inext (next)    | N/A
    ; TRAP        | Program (intentional)| Inext (next) | N/A
    ; FAULT      | Program (error)    | Icurr (retry)   | Maybe
    ; ABORT      | Hardware (fatal)   | Never returns   | No
    ;
    ; Key Difference: FAULT retries the current instruction
    ;                 Others skip past the triggering instruction

    LEA DX, newline
    INT 21h

    ; Exit
    MOV AH, 4Ch
    INT 21h

MAIN ENDP
END MAIN

annotations.text: INTERRUPT is asynchronous - hardware triggers it regardless of program flow
Timer interrupts occur even when program isn't doing anything - background events
TRAP is intentional - program deliberately executes INT to request service
System calls are TRAPs - interface between user program and OS kernel
FAULT can be recovered - handler fixes problem and retries SAME instruction
Page fault: page was on disk, handler loads it, program retries and succeeds
ABORT never returns - fatal error, OS terminates process immediately
Key distinction: FAULT returns to Icurr (retry), others return to Inext (skip)

commonMistakes: Confusing TRAP and INTERRUPT - TRAP is intentional, INTERRUPT is hardware-triggered
Thinking FAULT always aborts - FAULT can be recovered if handler fixes it
Forgetting that page fault is a FAULT not ABORT - memory was just on disk
Not understanding INT n is the instruction form of TRAP for system calls
Confusing INT (software exception/trap) with hardware interrupts - same mechanism
